My fasting blood sugar was 13 this morning. I'm really worried. What should I do?

That number is definitely on the high side, and I understand why you're concerned. With a fasting reading of 13 mmol/L (that's about 234 mg/dL), this is something you'll want your doctor to know about soon — don't wait for your next routine appointment.

In the meantime, here are some things that can help right now:
• Drink plenty of water — it helps your body flush out extra sugar through urine.
• Avoid anything sugary or high in carbs today — bread, rice, fruit juice, and sweets will push your numbers higher.
• A gentle walk after meals can help bring blood sugar down, but if you feel unwell or your numbers are very high, rest instead.
• Check your blood sugar again in a few hours to see which direction it's heading.

Watch for warning signs that need immediate medical attention: nausea, vomiting, fruity-smelling breath, or trouble breathing. If any of those appear, please go to the emergency room.

I know this feels scary, but one high reading doesn't mean everything is falling apart. Your doctor can help you figure out what caused the spike and adjust your plan. You're doing the right thing by paying attention to it.

What's glipizide? My pharmacy gave me this instead of my usual.

Glipizide is a diabetes medication that helps your pancreas release more insulin. It's in a class called sulfonylureas. The main thing to watch for with glipizide is low blood sugar, especially if you skip meals. Taking it about 30 minutes before eating typically works best. If the pharmacy gave you something different from your usual, it's worth double-checking with them to make sure there wasn't a mistake.

Quick question — is it normal to need less sleep as you get older? I used to sleep 8 hours, now I'm lucky to get 6.

It's a common belief that older adults need less sleep, but the research actually shows that sleep needs stay about the same throughout adulthood — around 7-8 hours. What changes is the ability to get that sleep in one continuous block.

As we age, we spend less time in deep sleep and more time in lighter sleep stages, which means we wake up more easily. The body's internal clock also shifts earlier — you might feel sleepy earlier in the evening and wake up earlier in the morning. This is normal, but it doesn't mean you need less total sleep.

The 6 hours you're getting may or may not be enough — the real question is how you feel during the day. If you're tired, nodding off, or having trouble concentrating, you probably need more. If you feel rested and functional on 6 hours, that might genuinely be enough for you.

If you're feeling sleep-deprived, focus on sleep quality more than quantity — a dark, cool, quiet bedroom and a consistent bedtime routine make the sleep you do get more restorative.

What is amlodipine used for?

Amlodipine is a calcium channel blocker that helps relax and widen your blood vessels, which lowers blood pressure. It's taken once a day. Some people notice mild ankle swelling when taking it — this is usually not dangerous, but if it bothers you or the swelling is significant, it's worth mentioning to your doctor.

Quick question — how much water should I actually drink at my age? I'm not very thirsty most days.

The sense of thirst does tend to diminish with age, so not feeling thirsty doesn't necessarily mean your body has enough fluid. Dehydration is actually quite common in older adults and can contribute to dizziness, confusion, constipation, and urinary tract infections.

A general guideline is about 6-8 cups (1.5-2 liters) of fluid per day, but this includes what you get from food and other drinks, not just plain water. Soups, fruits, vegetables, tea, and coffee all contribute.

Practical ways to get enough without forcing yourself to chug water:
• Have a glass of water with each meal and when taking medications — that alone covers a good portion.
• Keep a water bottle or cup visible throughout the day. You're more likely to sip if it's right there.
• Foods like cucumbers, watermelon, oranges, and yogurt have high water content.
• Herbal tea counts and can be more appealing than plain water, especially in the evening.

A simple way to check if you're well-hydrated is urine color — pale yellow is good, dark yellow or amber means you need more fluids.

One caution: if you have heart failure or kidney disease, your doctor may have given you a specific fluid restriction. Follow that rather than the general guideline.

what to do with hydrogen peroxide poisoning

Seek medical help right away. Do NOT make the person throw up unless poison control or a health care provider tells you to do so. If the chemical is on the skin or in the eyes, flush with lots of water for at least 15 minutes.

Before Calling Emergency
Have this information ready:

Person's age, weight, and condition
Name of the product (ingredients and strength, if known)
Time it was swallowed or got into eyes or on skin
Amount swallowed, in eyes, or on skin

I've been thinking about this — how do I improve my balance? I've noticed I'm a bit wobbly lately.

Balance naturally declines with age, but it's also very trainable — you can improve it at any age with consistent practice.

Simple exercises to do daily (near a wall or sturdy chair for support):
• Single-leg stand — hold onto a chair and lift one foot slightly off the ground. Start with 5-10 seconds and build up. Do this on each side.
• Heel-to-toe walking — walk in a straight line placing the heel of one foot directly in front of the toes of the other, like walking on a tightrope.
• Sit-to-stand — practice standing up from a chair without using your hands. This builds both leg strength and balance. Start with 5 repetitions.
• Side leg raises — stand behind a chair, hold on, and lift one leg out to the side. This strengthens hip muscles, which are important for stability.

Tai chi is one of the best-studied activities for improving balance in older adults — many community centers offer beginner classes.

Other things that affect balance: vision (when was your last eye check?), footwear (supportive, non-slip shoes help), and some medications (certain blood pressure drugs or sedatives can cause dizziness). If the wobbliness is new or getting worse, mention it to your doctor — there may be an underlying cause they can address.

Hey, can I ask you something? what kind of exercise is best for lowering blood sugar? I'm 68 and not very fit.

At 68, the best exercise is the one you'll actually do and enjoy — consistency matters much more than intensity for blood sugar management.

Walking is honestly one of the most effective options. Just 15-20 minutes after meals can make a noticeable difference in your post-meal blood sugar because your muscles use glucose directly from your bloodstream when they're active.
• Start with 10-15 minutes of walking after lunch or dinner, and build up gradually.
• Chair exercises are great if standing for long periods is hard — seated leg lifts, arm circles, and gentle marching in place all count.
• Tai chi and gentle yoga improve both physical fitness and balance, and many community centers offer classes specifically for seniors.
• Strength matters too — even light hand weights or resistance bands twice a week help your muscles become more responsive to insulin.

The most important thing is to start where you are. If 10 minutes feels like enough, that's your starting point. Check with your doctor before starting anything new, especially if you have heart concerns, but for most people, gentle daily movement is both safe and beneficial.

I've been wondering — is it okay to exercise if I have high blood pressure? I'm worried I'll make it worse.

Exercise is actually one of the best things you can do for high blood pressure — it strengthens your heart so it can pump blood with less effort, which reduces the force on your arteries. Regular aerobic exercise can lower your systolic pressure by 5-8 points.

The key is starting gently and being consistent:
• Walking, swimming, and cycling are excellent choices — low-impact but effective.
• Start with 15-20 minutes and build up slowly over weeks, not days.
• Avoid holding your breath when lifting anything — this can cause a temporary blood pressure spike. Exhale during the effort.
• If you get very short of breath, feel dizzy, or have chest pain, stop and rest. These are signs to ease off.

Isometric exercises (like heavy weightlifting or plank holds) can temporarily spike blood pressure more than walking or swimming, so those are worth being more cautious with.

As always, check with your doctor before starting, but for the vast majority of people with hypertension, regular moderate exercise is not just safe — it's recommended.

I've been wondering — what foods actually help lower cholesterol? I've heard oatmeal helps.

Oatmeal does help, and the reason is soluble fiber — it binds to cholesterol in your digestive system and carries it out of your body before it can get into your bloodstream. A bowl of oatmeal (not the sugary instant kind) gives you about 2-3 grams of soluble fiber, and aiming for 5-10 grams daily can lower LDL cholesterol by 5-10 points.

Other foods with solid evidence behind them:
• Barley, beans, lentils, and peas — also rich in soluble fiber.
• Nuts, especially almonds and walnuts — a small handful a day can improve cholesterol. Just don't overdo it since they're calorie-dense.
• Fatty fish like salmon, mackerel, and sardines — the omega-3s don't directly lower LDL but they reduce inflammation and triglycerides and are good for overall heart health.
• Avocados and olive oil — sources of healthy monounsaturated fats. Replacing butter and margarine with olive oil is a simple swap that adds up.
• Foods fortified with plant sterols/stanols (some margarines and yogurts) — these block cholesterol absorption in the gut.

What to reduce: saturated fats (fatty meat, butter, cheese, coconut oil) and trans fats (anything with 'partially hydrogenated' oil in the ingredients).

The Mediterranean diet pattern combines all of these — it's one of the most studied and effective eating patterns for heart health.

Quick question — my doctor said my blood pressure is 145 over 90. Is that really that bad at my age?

A reading of 145/90 is considered stage 1 hypertension. At any age, blood pressure consistently in this range puts extra strain on your heart, blood vessels, and kidneys over time. It increases the risk of stroke, heart attack, and kidney problems.

However, treatment decisions for older adults are more nuanced than just looking at a number. Your doctor considers your overall health, other conditions, risk of falls (if blood pressure drops too low from medication), and quality of life.

For many people in their 60s and 70s, a target of below 140/90 is reasonable, and for some, below 130/80 if tolerated well. But if you have issues with dizziness or falls, a slightly higher target may be safer.

The good news is that 145/90 is often very responsive to lifestyle changes — cutting back on salt, walking more, and losing even a modest amount of weight can bring it down meaningfully. Your doctor is flagging this now so you have a chance to address it before it causes problems.

Fruits like mangoes and bananas are on the sweeter, higher-carb side, but that doesn't mean they're off limits. Try these approaches:
• Have a smaller portion — half a banana or a few slices of mango instead of the whole fruit.
• Pair fruit with a bit of protein or healthy fat — a small handful of nuts or a spoonful of plain yogurt slows down how fast the sugar hits your bloodstream.
• Eat fruit as part of a meal rather than on an empty stomach.
• Timing matters — your body handles carbs better earlier in the day and after physical activity.

Lower-sugar fruits like berries, cherries, and green apples are a bit easier on blood sugar if you want something you can eat more of. But a little mango now and then, enjoyed mindfully, is part of a balanced life, not a failure.

Quick question — how do I know if I'm getting enough protein? I don't eat much meat anymore.

Protein needs actually increase somewhat with age because the body becomes less efficient at using protein to maintain muscle. The general recommendation for older adults is about 1.0-1.2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight per day — for a 70 kg person, that's roughly 70-85 grams.

You can absolutely meet your protein needs without much meat. Here are excellent non-meat sources:
• Eggs — about 6 grams of protein each, and they contain all essential amino acids.
• Greek yogurt — roughly 15-20 grams per cup, plus calcium for bones.
• Cottage cheese — about 14 grams per half-cup.
• Beans, lentils, and chickpeas — about 7-9 grams per half-cup cooked, plus lots of fiber.
• Tofu and tempeh — 10-15 grams per serving.
• Fish, if you eat it — a small fillet of salmon or cod gives 20-25 grams plus omega-3s.
• Nuts and seeds — a handful gives about 5-7 grams (but they're calorie-dense, so moderate).

Signs you might not be getting enough protein: wounds healing slowly, feeling weaker than expected, losing muscle, hair thinning more than usual.

A simple approach is to include a protein source at each meal — an egg at breakfast, beans or fish at lunch, yogurt as a snack, and some tofu or lentils at dinner. Spreading it throughout the day is more effective than getting it all at once.

I saw something online and wanted to check — is it safe to have a glass of wine if I take metformin?

An occasional small glass of wine is usually fine for most people taking metformin, but there are a few important things to know. Alcohol can increase the risk of a rare but serious side effect called lactic acidosis, especially if you drink heavily or binge drink. Alcohol can also affect blood sugar — it can cause it to drop hours after drinking. Having food with your drink and keeping it moderate are wise precautions.

What kind of exercise is best for lowering blood sugar? I'm 68 and not very fit.

At 68, the best exercise is the one you'll actually do and enjoy — consistency matters much more than intensity for blood sugar management.

Walking is honestly one of the most effective options. Just 15-20 minutes after meals can make a noticeable difference in your post-meal blood sugar because your muscles use glucose directly from your bloodstream when they're active.
• Start with 10-15 minutes of walking after lunch or dinner, and build up gradually.
• Chair exercises are great if standing for long periods is hard — seated leg lifts, arm circles, and gentle marching in place all count.
• Tai chi and gentle yoga improve both physical fitness and balance, and many community centers offer classes specifically for seniors.
• Strength matters too — even light hand weights or resistance bands twice a week help your muscles become more responsive to insulin.

The most important thing is to start where you are. If 10 minutes feels like enough, that's your starting point. Check with your doctor before starting anything new, especially if you have heart concerns, but for most people, gentle daily movement is both safe and beneficial.

I read something and wanted to ask — is it okay to exercise if I have high blood pressure? I'm worried I'll make it worse.

Exercise is actually one of the best things you can do for high blood pressure — it strengthens your heart so it can pump blood with less effort, which reduces the force on your arteries. Regular aerobic exercise can lower your systolic pressure by 5-8 points.

The key is starting gently and being consistent:
• Walking, swimming, and cycling are excellent choices — low-impact but effective.
• Start with 15-20 minutes and build up slowly over weeks, not days.
• Avoid holding your breath when lifting anything — this can cause a temporary blood pressure spike. Exhale during the effort.
• If you get very short of breath, feel dizzy, or have chest pain, stop and rest. These are signs to ease off.

Isometric exercises (like heavy weightlifting or plank holds) can temporarily spike blood pressure more than walking or swimming, so those are worth being more cautious with.

As always, check with your doctor before starting, but for the vast majority of people with hypertension, regular moderate exercise is not just safe — it's recommended.

Hey, can I ask you something? what should my blood sugar be before breakfast? I'm never sure if my numbers are okay.

For most older adults with diabetes, a reasonable fasting target (before breakfast) is around 4.4 to 7.2 mmol/L (80 to 130 mg/dL). But targets vary from person to person based on your age, how long you've had diabetes, other health conditions, and your risk of low blood sugar.

Your doctor may have set a different target for you specifically. Some doctors prefer slightly higher targets for older adults to reduce the risk of dangerous low blood sugar episodes.

Generally:
• Below 4.0 mmol/L (72 mg/dL) — too low, treat with fast-acting sugar like juice or glucose tablets.
• 4.4 – 7.2 mmol/L (80–130 mg/dL) — reasonable fasting range for most.
• Above 10.0 mmol/L (180 mg/dL) consistently — worth discussing with your doctor.

The best thing you can do is ask at your next appointment: 'What's the fasting range you want me to aim for?' Having a clear target from your own doctor will give you much more confidence when you check your numbers.

What does 'off-label' mean? My doctor prescribed something and said it's off-label.

Off-label means a medication is being used for a purpose that wasn't in its original FDA approval. This is actually quite common and perfectly legal. For example, gabapentin was approved for seizures but is often prescribed for nerve pain. It doesn't mean the use is unsafe — there's usually research supporting it — but the manufacturer just didn't go through the formal approval process for that particular use.

messages.content: You are XiaoNuan, a warm companion for elderly users. Your role is to listen, comfort, and keep them company. Speak naturally and gently, like a caring friend — not a doctor, not a dispatcher, not a secretary. When a user shares a worry or discomfort, acknowledge their feeling first and sit with them in the moment. You may provide general health and medication information when the user asks, but never give personal medical advice or diagnosis. If a situation sounds genuinely serious, gently suggest they consider checking with a doctor — phrase it as a soft suggestion, never a command. Avoid imperative language like 'Do not', 'You must', or 'Call immediately'. Vary your wording and sentence structure across responses. Do not reuse the same phrases or templates — each reply should feel fresh.
I've been meaning to ask about this — what's glipizide? My pharmacy gave me this instead of my usual.

Glipizide is a diabetes medication that helps your pancreas release more insulin. It's in a class called sulfonylureas. The main thing to watch for with glipizide is low blood sugar, especially if you skip meals. Taking it about 30 minutes before eating typically works best. If the pharmacy gave you something different from your usual, it's worth double-checking with them to make sure there wasn't a mistake.

can you take amoxicillin with hydrocodone ?

There are no direct drug interactions between Norco (acetaminophen, hydrocodone), amoxicillin (Amoxil) and Adipex (phentermine). Take the Norco only as needed. This may cause drowsiness, be careful if you must be alert. Take the amoxicillin until finished.

You are XiaoNuan, a warm and patient companion assistant for older adults. You may use reminder tools only when the user explicitly asks to create, query, update, or delete reminders. Do not call tools for normal chatting, emotional support, life sharing, or statements that merely mention time. Do not fabricate tool results. You can confirm success only after receiving a role=tool result. If the tool returns missing_fields, ask naturally for the missing information. If the tool returns ambiguous, ask the user to choose the specific reminder. If the tool returns not_found, explain that no matching reminder was found and offer a next step.
Delete my reminder to water the plants this evening.
{"state": "success", "status": "success"}
Done — I deleted your reminder to water the plants this evening.

Can I ask you something? what is clopidogrel? I take it along with aspirin.

Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet medication — it helps stop blood cells called platelets from sticking together and forming clots. Taking it with aspirin is called dual antiplatelet therapy, and it's often prescribed after someone has had a stent placed or a heart attack. The combination is usually taken for a specific period of time determined by your cardiologist.

What is alendronate? My doctor says I need it for my bones.

Alendronate is a bisphosphonate that helps strengthen bones and reduce the risk of fractures in people with osteoporosis. There's a specific way to take it: first thing in the morning with a full glass of plain water, and you need to stay upright — sitting or standing — for at least 30 minutes afterward. You shouldn't eat, drink anything other than water, or take other medications during that time. This routine helps the medicine work properly and protects your esophagus.

how do steroids effect the digestive system

Corticosteroid use was associated with increased risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and perforation. The increased risk was statistically significant for hospitalised patients only. For patients in ambulatory care, the total occurrence of bleeding or perforation was very low, and the increased risk was not statistically significant.

Can medications cause depression? I've been feeling low since starting a new one.

Yes, some medications can affect mood as a side effect. Beta blockers, some steroids, certain Parkinson's medications, and even some blood pressure drugs have been associated with mood changes or depression in some people. If the timing lines up with starting a new medication, it's really worth mentioning to your doctor — sometimes switching to a different medication in the same class can make a difference.

Hey, can I ask you something? my doctor said my blood pressure is 145 over 90. Is that really that bad at my age?

A reading of 145/90 is considered stage 1 hypertension. At any age, blood pressure consistently in this range puts extra strain on your heart, blood vessels, and kidneys over time. It increases the risk of stroke, heart attack, and kidney problems.

However, treatment decisions for older adults are more nuanced than just looking at a number. Your doctor considers your overall health, other conditions, risk of falls (if blood pressure drops too low from medication), and quality of life.

For many people in their 60s and 70s, a target of below 140/90 is reasonable, and for some, below 130/80 if tolerated well. But if you have issues with dizziness or falls, a slightly higher target may be safer.

The good news is that 145/90 is often very responsive to lifestyle changes — cutting back on salt, walking more, and losing even a modest amount of weight can bring it down meaningfully. Your doctor is flagging this now so you have a chance to address it before it causes problems.

how long does marijuana stay in your system

Marijuana, also known as cannabis or weed, is usually detectable in bodily fluids for 1 to 30 days after last use. As with other drugs, it may be detectable in hair for several months.

Marijuana detection windows depend on how much you smoke or ingest, as well as how often. In general, higher doses and more frequent use are associated with longer detection times.

For daily users, cannabis may be detectable for several months after last use. The longest-reported detection times are more than 90 days.

how is cocaine and amphetamine used

Cocaine is a type of drug that functions to increase the availability of the neurotransmitter dopamine in the brain. Dopamine is associated with the generation of 'euphoric' emotions, the regulation of movement, and the processing of reward cues. However, it is also associated with a considerable potential for dependence and abuse. Cocaine abuse is related to an increased risk of:    Psychiatric disorders.    Disease.    Death.  Cocaine is attractive as a recreational substance due to the perceived positive effects on mood, motivation, and energy. Someone abusing cocaine may smoke, snort, or take it intravenously (via injection).

what is fluoride

The mineral fluoride occurs naturally on earth and is released from rocks into the soil, water, and air. All water contains some fluoride. Usually, the fluoride level in water is not enough to prevent tooth decay; however, some groundwater and natural springs can have naturally high levels of fluoride. Fluoride has been proven to protect teeth from decay. Bacteria in the mouth produce acid when a person eats sugary foods. This acid eats away minerals from the tooth’s surface, making the tooth weaker and increasing the chance of developing cavities. Fluoride helps to rebuild and strengthen the tooth’s surface, or enamel. Water fluoridation prevents tooth decay by providing frequent and consistent contact with low levels of fluoride. By keeping the tooth strong and solid, fluoride stops cavities from forming and can even rebuild the tooth’s surface.

how does amxacillin clavulanic acid work

The combination of amoxicillin and clavulanic acid is used to treat certain infections caused by bacteria, including infections of the ears, lungs, sinus, skin, and urinary tract. Amoxicillin is in a class of medications called penicillin-like antibiotics. It works by stopping the growth of bacteria. Clavulanic acid is in a class of medications called beta-lactamase inhibitors. It works by preventing bacteria from destroying amoxicillin.

Quick question — what's glipizide? My pharmacy gave me this instead of my usual.

Glipizide is a diabetes medication that helps your pancreas release more insulin. It's in a class called sulfonylureas. The main thing to watch for with glipizide is low blood sugar, especially if you skip meals. Taking it about 30 minutes before eating typically works best. If the pharmacy gave you something different from your usual, it's worth double-checking with them to make sure there wasn't a mistake.

what if i quit taking thyroid medicine

Thyroid helps control the symptoms of hypothyroidism, but does not cure this condition. It may take up to several weeks before you notice any change in your symptoms. To control the symptoms of hypothyroidism, you probably will need to take thyroid for the rest of your life. Continue to take thyroid even if you feel well. Do not stop taking thyroid without talking to your doctor.

Hey, can I ask you something? i want to start exercising but I'm afraid of falling. What's the safest way to begin?

That's a really valid concern — fear of falling is one of the biggest barriers to staying active for many older adults. But here's the thing: appropriate exercise actually reduces your fall risk by improving strength, balance, and coordination.

The safest starting points:
• Chair-based exercises — seated marches, leg lifts, and arm movements. You can find free videos on YouTube specifically for seniors. These build strength with zero fall risk.
• Walking with support — start in your own home or hallway, where you can hold onto walls or furniture. Even 5 minutes of walking back and forth counts.
• Using a stationary bike — no balance required, and you can go as slowly as you want.
• Balance exercises near a wall or sturdy chair — standing on one foot for a few seconds while holding onto something, then building up.

Consider asking your doctor for a physical therapy referral. A PT can assess your specific balance issues, give you a tailored exercise program, and teach you how to get up from the floor safely — which ironically reduces the fear of falling because you know what to do if it happens.

Start with just 5-10 minutes and see how you feel. The goal isn't to become an athlete — it's to maintain the strength and balance to do the things you enjoy.

Hey, can I ask you something? my cholesterol is a little high but my doctor hasn't put me on medication. Should I be worried?

If your doctor isn't prescribing medication yet, it likely means your overall risk isn't in the range where medication clearly provides more benefit than risk. Doctors look at cholesterol numbers in the context of everything else — your age, blood pressure, whether you smoke, and whether you have diabetes.

A 'little high' LDL might be something your doctor believes can be improved with diet and exercise first, which is a completely reasonable approach. Lifestyle changes can lower LDL by 10-20% for many people.

That said, it's absolutely fair to ask your doctor directly: 'Based on my numbers and overall health, what's my risk of a heart attack or stroke in the next 10 years?' This is called a cardiovascular risk assessment, and it's a standard calculation doctors can do. The answer will tell you how aggressively you need to manage your cholesterol.

In the meantime, focusing on the dietary changes we discussed — more soluble fiber, healthy fats instead of saturated fats, and regular walking — is a good foundation regardless of whether you ever need medication.

This is an important combination to be careful with. Ibuprofen and other NSAIDs can increase the risk of bleeding when taken with warfarin, because both affect how the blood clots. Many doctors recommend avoiding NSAIDs altogether when you're on warfarin. Acetaminophen is often suggested as a safer alternative for pain, but even that should be discussed with your doctor if you're on warfarin.

can I drink coffee with my thyroid medication in the morning?

when to take loratadine

Hay fever typically worsens at night and feels most severe in the morning, when levels of symptom-triggering histamine are highest. Once-daily antihistamines, such as Claritin, reach their peak eight to 12 hours after you take them, so using them at dinnertime means better control of morning symptoms. (Take twice-a-day antihistamines in the morning and evening.)

Long-term use of proton pump inhibitors like omeprazole has been associated with some potential risks — such as reduced absorption of certain nutrients like magnesium, calcium, and vitamin B12, and a slightly higher risk of bone fractures. how often is the tetanus shot "given?"

Everyone needs tetanus vaccines throughout their lives. That means everyone needs to get vaccinated as babies, children, and adults.

Infants and children birth through age 6
Young children need the DTaP vaccine as part of their routine vaccine schedule. Young children need a dose of the vaccine at:

2 months
4 months
6 months
15 through 18 months
4 through 6 years
If your child has had a serious reaction to the whooping cough part of the DTaP vaccine, they may be able to get the DT vaccine instead. 
Preteens and teens ages 7 through 18
Older children need 1 booster shot of the Tdap vaccine at age 11 or 12 as part of their routine vaccine schedule.
Adults age 19 and older
Adults need 1 booster shot of the Td vaccine every 10 years as part of their routine vaccine schedule. If you get a deep cut or a burn, you may need the booster earlier — especially if the cut or burn is dirty.

If you missed the Tdap booster as a teen, you’ll need to get a Tdap booster instead to make sure you have protection from whooping cough.

Pregnant women
Pregnant women need 1 booster shot of the Tdap vaccine during the third trimester of each pregnancy.

I read something and wanted to ask — is it normal to need less sleep as you get older? I used to sleep 8 hours, now I'm lucky to get 6.

It's a common belief that older adults need less sleep, but the research actually shows that sleep needs stay about the same throughout adulthood — around 7-8 hours. What changes is the ability to get that sleep in one continuous block.

As we age, we spend less time in deep sleep and more time in lighter sleep stages, which means we wake up more easily. The body's internal clock also shifts earlier — you might feel sleepy earlier in the evening and wake up earlier in the morning. This is normal, but it doesn't mean you need less total sleep.

The 6 hours you're getting may or may not be enough — the real question is how you feel during the day. If you're tired, nodding off, or having trouble concentrating, you probably need more. If you feel rested and functional on 6 hours, that might genuinely be enough for you.

If you're feeling sleep-deprived, focus on sleep quality more than quantity — a dark, cool, quiet bedroom and a consistent bedtime routine make the sleep you do get more restorative.

I've been thinking about this — is heat or ice better for arthritis pain? I hear different things.

They both help, but for different situations:

Heat is best for stiffness, especially in the morning or before activity:
• A warm shower or bath first thing helps loosen up stiff joints.
• A heating pad or warm towel applied for 15-20 minutes relaxes muscles and increases blood flow.
• Use heat before exercise or activity to make movement easier.

Ice (cold) is best for acute pain and swelling, especially after activity:
• Apply an ice pack or bag of frozen peas wrapped in a thin towel for 10-15 minutes.
• Use cold after you've been on your feet for a while or if a joint feels hot and swollen.
• Cold numbs pain and reduces inflammation.

Some people do well with contrast therapy — alternating warm and cool — to get both benefits.

Always put a cloth between your skin and the hot or cold source to protect your skin, and limit sessions to about 15-20 minutes at a time.

I saw something online and wanted to check — what is alendronate? My doctor says I need it for my bones.

Alendronate is a bisphosphonate that helps strengthen bones and reduce the risk of fractures in people with osteoporosis. There's a specific way to take it: first thing in the morning with a full glass of plain water, and you need to stay upright — sitting or standing — for at least 30 minutes afterward. You shouldn't eat, drink anything other than water, or take other medications during that time. This routine helps the medicine work properly and protects your esophagus.

i get anxious about my health all the time. Every little ache makes me think something is terribly wrong.

Health anxiety is really common, especially as we get older and become more aware of our bodies — and more aware of what can go wrong. It's exhausting to live with that constant background worry.

Some things that may help:
• Set a 'worry window' — give yourself 10-15 minutes a day to acknowledge all your health worries, write them down if that helps, then deliberately close that window and redirect your attention. When worries pop up at other times, tell yourself 'I'll think about this during my worry time.'
• Limit internet searching — it almost always leads to the most alarming possibilities. If you have a symptom, write it down for your next doctor's visit instead of googling it.
• Keep a symptom log with a rule — only record things that persist for more than a few days or that your doctor specifically asked you to track. This prevents you from obsessively monitoring every sensation.
• Practice grounding — when anxiety spikes, try the 5-4-3-2-1 technique: name 5 things you can see, 4 you can touch, 3 you can hear, 2 you can smell, and 1 you can taste. It brings your attention back to the present.
• Talk to your doctor about the anxiety itself — health anxiety is a real condition and it's treatable. Sometimes just naming it to your doctor changes how they communicate with you.

It's completely understandable to worry about your health. But when the worry itself starts harming your quality of life more than the things you're worrying about, it deserves attention in its own right.

what can I do about stress? I feel tense all the time and I carry it in my shoulders.

Chronic stress isn't just unpleasant — it affects blood pressure, sleep, digestion, and immune function. The shoulder and neck tension you're describing is one of the most common physical manifestations.

Quick relief for shoulder tension:
• Shoulder rolls — lift your shoulders toward your ears, roll them back, and drop them. Do this 5-10 times throughout the day.
• Heat — a heating pad or warm compress on the neck and shoulders for 15 minutes can release tight muscles.
• Progressive muscle relaxation — starting from your feet and working up, tense each muscle group for 5 seconds, then release. By the time you reach your shoulders, you'll notice a real difference.

Longer-term stress management:
• Deep breathing — even 2-3 minutes of slow, diaphragmatic breathing (belly breathing, not chest breathing) shifts your nervous system out of fight-or-flight mode. Inhale for 4 counts, exhale for 6.
• Nature — even looking at trees or sitting in a garden for 15 minutes can measurably reduce cortisol levels.
• Social connection — stress feels heavier when you carry it alone. A phone call with a friend, a visit with a neighbor, or even chatting here can lighten the load.
• Gentle exercise — walking, stretching, or tai chi are all excellent for burning off stress hormones.

If you find yourself feeling tense and wound up most days despite trying these things, it might be worth talking to your doctor. Chronic stress sometimes needs more structured support, and there's no shame in that.

i live alone and cooking for one feels like too much effort. I end up eating toast or nothing.

This is such a common challenge, and it's completely understandable — the motivation to cook often comes from sharing the meal with someone. But your body still needs good fuel even when you're eating solo.

Some ideas that might make it feel less burdensome:
• Cook a proper meal once or twice a week and portion out leftovers. A pot of soup, a casserole, or roasted vegetables and chicken can provide several meals with one effort.
• Batch-prep components rather than full meals — cook a pot of quinoa or brown rice, roast a tray of vegetables, and grill some chicken. Then mix and match through the week.
• Frozen vegetables are just as nutritious as fresh and don't go bad. You can take out exactly what you need.
• Keep some easy, nutritious fallbacks: eggs (quick protein), canned beans (fiber and protein), Greek yogurt, pre-washed salad greens.
• Sometimes just making the table setting nice — a placemat, a napkin, some music — can make eating alone feel more like a proper meal and less like an afterthought.

Toast isn't the worst thing in the world, but adding an egg on top or some avocado turns it into something that will sustain you much longer. You deserve to eat well, even when it's just you.

I've been thinking about this — my doctor said my blood pressure is 145 over 90. Is that really that bad at my age?

A reading of 145/90 is considered stage 1 hypertension. At any age, blood pressure consistently in this range puts extra strain on your heart, blood vessels, and kidneys over time. It increases the risk of stroke, heart attack, and kidney problems.

However, treatment decisions for older adults are more nuanced than just looking at a number. Your doctor considers your overall health, other conditions, risk of falls (if blood pressure drops too low from medication), and quality of life.

For many people in their 60s and 70s, a target of below 140/90 is reasonable, and for some, below 130/80 if tolerated well. But if you have issues with dizziness or falls, a slightly higher target may be safer.

The good news is that 145/90 is often very responsive to lifestyle changes — cutting back on salt, walking more, and losing even a modest amount of weight can bring it down meaningfully. Your doctor is flagging this now so you have a chance to address it before it causes problems.

how much water should I actually drink at my age? I'm not very thirsty most days.

The sense of thirst does tend to diminish with age, so not feeling thirsty doesn't necessarily mean your body has enough fluid. Dehydration is actually quite common in older adults and can contribute to dizziness, confusion, constipation, and urinary tract infections.

A general guideline is about 6-8 cups (1.5-2 liters) of fluid per day, but this includes what you get from food and other drinks, not just plain water. Soups, fruits, vegetables, tea, and coffee all contribute.

Practical ways to get enough without forcing yourself to chug water:
• Have a glass of water with each meal and when taking medications — that alone covers a good portion.
• Keep a water bottle or cup visible throughout the day. You're more likely to sip if it's right there.
• Foods like cucumbers, watermelon, oranges, and yogurt have high water content.
• Herbal tea counts and can be more appealing than plain water, especially in the evening.

A simple way to check if you're well-hydrated is urine color — pale yellow is good, dark yellow or amber means you need more fluids.

One caution: if you have heart failure or kidney disease, your doctor may have given you a specific fluid restriction. Follow that rather than the general guideline.

Can I take acetaminophen every day for my arthritis?

Acetaminophen can be helpful for arthritis pain, and many people use it regularly. The important thing is to stay within the recommended daily amount — usually no more than 3000mg per day for older adults. Too much can affect the liver, so if you're taking it every day for a long time, it's a good idea to check in with your doctor every so often to make sure it's still the right choice for you.

how xarelto affects in the process of homeostasis

XARELTO is a selective inhibitor of FXa. It does not require a cofactor (such as Anti-thrombin III) for activity. Rivaroxaban inhibits free FXa and prothrombinase activity. Rivaroxaban has no direct effect on platelet aggregation, but indirectly inhibits platelet aggregation induced by thrombin. By inhibiting FXa, rivaroxaban decreases thrombin generation.

Quick question — what foods actually help lower cholesterol? I've heard oatmeal helps.

Oatmeal does help, and the reason is soluble fiber — it binds to cholesterol in your digestive system and carries it out of your body before it can get into your bloodstream. A bowl of oatmeal (not the sugary instant kind) gives you about 2-3 grams of soluble fiber, and aiming for 5-10 grams daily can lower LDL cholesterol by 5-10 points.

Other foods with solid evidence behind them:
• Barley, beans, lentils, and peas — also rich in soluble fiber.
• Nuts, especially almonds and walnuts — a small handful a day can improve cholesterol. Just don't overdo it since they're calorie-dense.
• Fatty fish like salmon, mackerel, and sardines — the omega-3s don't directly lower LDL but they reduce inflammation and triglycerides and are good for overall heart health.
• Avocados and olive oil — sources of healthy monounsaturated fats. Replacing butter and margarine with olive oil is a simple swap that adds up.
• Foods fortified with plant sterols/stanols (some margarines and yogurts) — these block cholesterol absorption in the gut.

What to reduce: saturated fats (fatty meat, butter, cheese, coconut oil) and trans fats (anything with 'partially hydrogenated' oil in the ingredients).

The Mediterranean diet pattern combines all of these — it's one of the most studied and effective eating patterns for heart health.

I take so many supplements but I'm not sure any of them actually help. How do I know?

You're asking a really good question, and honestly, many people are in the same boat — taking supplements out of habit without knowing if they're making a difference.

A few things to consider:
• Supplements are not regulated the same way medications are. The quality and actual content can vary a lot between brands.
• Some supplements can interact with prescription medications in significant ways. St. John's Wort, for example, interferes with many drugs. Even 'natural' doesn't mean harmless.
• Vitamin D and B12 are two supplements that actually have good evidence for many older adults, and your doctor can check your blood levels to know if you truly need them. Calcium may be helpful for bone health but recent research has raised questions about supplementing high doses.

The best approach is to bring all your supplements — bottles and all — to your next doctor's appointment and go through them one by one. Ask: 'Is there evidence this helps someone in my situation? Could any of these interact with my medications? Are there any I can stop taking?'

A good pharmacist can also review your supplements alongside your medications. You might find you can simplify your routine and save money without losing any real benefit.

I've been wondering — how do I know if I'm getting enough protein? I don't eat much meat anymore.

Protein needs actually increase somewhat with age because the body becomes less efficient at using protein to maintain muscle. The general recommendation for older adults is about 1.0-1.2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight per day — for a 70 kg person, that's roughly 70-85 grams.

You can absolutely meet your protein needs without much meat. Here are excellent non-meat sources:
• Eggs — about 6 grams of protein each, and they contain all essential amino acids.
• Greek yogurt — roughly 15-20 grams per cup, plus calcium for bones.
• Cottage cheese — about 14 grams per half-cup.
• Beans, lentils, and chickpeas — about 7-9 grams per half-cup cooked, plus lots of fiber.
• Tofu and tempeh — 10-15 grams per serving.
• Fish, if you eat it — a small fillet of salmon or cod gives 20-25 grams plus omega-3s.
• Nuts and seeds — a handful gives about 5-7 grams (but they're calorie-dense, so moderate).

Signs you might not be getting enough protein: wounds healing slowly, feeling weaker than expected, losing muscle, hair thinning more than usual.

A simple approach is to include a protein source at each meal — an egg at breakfast, beans or fish at lunch, yogurt as a snack, and some tofu or lentils at dinner. Spreading it throughout the day is more effective than getting it all at once.

I read something and wanted to ask — are there any natural remedies for chronic pain that actually work?

Some natural approaches do have evidence behind them for chronic pain management. They're generally not as potent as medications for acute relief, but they can be effective as part of a broader management plan:

• Turmeric/curcumin — has anti-inflammatory properties. The challenge is absorption; products with black pepper extract (piperine) are better absorbed. It can interact with blood thinners, so check with your pharmacist if you're on warfarin or similar.
• Omega-3 fatty acids (fish oil) — have anti-inflammatory effects and there's reasonable evidence for helping with rheumatoid arthritis pain specifically.
• Ginger — also has anti-inflammatory compounds and some people find it helpful for osteoarthritis pain.
• Capsaicin cream — made from chili peppers, applied to the skin over a painful joint. It depletes substance P, a pain-signaling chemical. It causes a burning sensation initially but can provide significant relief after a week or two of consistent use.
• Mindfulness meditation and gentle yoga — strong evidence for reducing the suffering component of chronic pain, even when the pain sensation itself doesn't change.

Important: 'natural' doesn't automatically mean safe, especially when combined with prescription medications. Always tell your doctor and pharmacist about any supplements you're taking or considering.

I read something and wanted to ask — my feet feel numb and tingly sometimes. Is that related to my diabetes?

That tingling or numbness could be peripheral neuropathy, which is a common complication of diabetes — especially if blood sugar has been running high for a while. High blood sugar can damage the small nerves, particularly in the feet and hands.

Please mention this to your doctor at your next visit, or sooner if it's getting worse. In the meantime:
• Check your feet daily for any cuts, blisters, or red spots — if you can't feel them, a small injury can get worse without you noticing.
• Wear well-fitting shoes and never go barefoot, even indoors.
• Keep your feet clean and moisturized, but don't put lotion between your toes.
• Keeping blood sugar in a healthier range can slow or even reverse some of the nerve symptoms over time.

Good foot care becomes really important with diabetes, but it's something you can manage well with daily attention and good habits.

Quick question — can I still eat fruit if I have diabetes? I love mangoes and bananas.

You can absolutely still enjoy fruit — having diabetes doesn't mean you have to give up the foods you love. The key is choosing wisely and managing portions.

Fruits like mangoes and bananas are on the sweeter, higher-carb side, but that doesn't mean they're off limits. Try these approaches:
• Have a smaller portion — half a banana or a few slices of mango instead of the whole fruit.
• Pair fruit with a bit of protein or healthy fat — a small handful of nuts or a spoonful of plain yogurt slows down how fast the sugar hits your bloodstream.
• Eat fruit as part of a meal rather than on an empty stomach.
• Timing matters — your body handles carbs better earlier in the day and after physical activity.

Lower-sugar fruits like berries, cherries, and green apples are a bit easier on blood sugar if you want something you can eat more of. But a little mango now and then, enjoyed mindfully, is part of a balanced life, not a failure.

amiodarone for afibwhat are side effects

Adverse reactions have been very common in virtually all series of patients treated with amiodarone for ventricular arrhythmias with relatively large doses of drug (400 mg/day and above), occurring in about three-fourths of all patients and causing discontinuation in 7 to 18%. The most serious reactions are pulmonary toxicity, exacerbation of arrhythmia, and rare serious liver injury (see “WARNINGS”), but other adverse effects constitute important problems. They are often reversible with dose reduction or cessation of amiodarone treatment. Most of the adverse effects appear to become more frequent with continued treatment beyond six months, although rates appear to remain relatively constant beyond one year. The time and dose relationships of adverse effects are under continued study.

Neurologic problems are extremely common, occurring in 20 to 40% of patients and including malaise and fatigue, tremor and involuntary movements, poor coordination and gait, and peripheral neuropathy; they are rarely a reason to stop therapy and may respond to dose reductions or discontinuation (see “PRECAUTIONS”). There have been spontaneous reports of demyelinating polyneuropathy.

Gastrointestinal complaints, most commonly nausea, vomiting, constipation, and anorexia, occur in about 25% of patients but rarely require discontinuation of drug. These commonly occur during high-dose administration (i.e., loading dose) and usually respond to dose reduction or divided doses.

Ophthalmic abnormalities including optic neuropathy and/or optic neuritis, in some cases progressing to permanent blindness, papilledema, corneal degeneration, photosensitivity, eye discomfort, scotoma, lens opacities, and macular degeneration have been reported (see “WARNINGS”).

Asymptomatic corneal microdeposits are present in virtually all adult patients who have been on drug for more than 6 months. Some patients develop eye symptoms of halos, photophobia, and dry eyes. Vision is rarely affected and drug discontinuation is rarely needed.

Dermatological adverse reactions occur in about 15% of patients, with photosensitivity being most common (about 10%). Sunscreen and protection from sun exposure may be helpful, and drug discontinuation is not usually necessary. Prolonged exposure to amiodarone occasionally results in a blue-gray pigmentation. This is slowly and occasionally incompletely reversible on discontinuation of drug but is of cosmetic importance only.

Cardiovascular adverse reactions, other than exacerbation of the arrhythmias, include the uncommon occurrence of congestive heart failure (3%) and bradycardia. Bradycardia usually responds to dosage reduction but may require a pacemaker for control. CHF rarely requires drug discontinuation. Cardiac conduction abnormalities occur infrequently and are reversible on discontinuation of drug.

what does 'off-label' mean? My doctor prescribed something and said it's off-label.

Off-label means a medication is being used for a purpose that wasn't in its original FDA approval. This is actually quite common and perfectly legal. For example, gabapentin was approved for seizures but is often prescribed for nerve pain. It doesn't mean the use is unsafe — there's usually research supporting it — but the manufacturer just didn't go through the formal approval process for that particular use.

is it true that bitter melon can help lower blood sugar? My neighbor swears by it.

Bitter melon does contain compounds that may help lower blood sugar — there's actually some research behind it. It has substances that work a bit like insulin in the body. But there are a few important cautions:

• Bitter melon can interact with diabetes medications, especially insulin and sulfonylureas like glipizide, potentially causing blood sugar to drop too low.
• The effect varies a lot from person to person, so it's not something you can rely on as a replacement for your prescribed treatment.
• It can cause digestive upset in some people.

If you want to try it, please talk to your doctor or pharmacist first — they need to know because they may want to monitor your blood sugar more closely or adjust your medication dose. Think of it as something that might complement your current plan, not replace it.

I take metoprolol every morning. What is it for exactly?

Metoprolol is a beta blocker, which means it slows down your heart rate and reduces how hard your heart has to work. It's commonly used for high blood pressure, angina, or after a heart attack. Because it lowers heart rate, some people feel a bit more tired than usual when they first start taking it. It comes in two forms — an immediate-release version taken twice a day, and an extended-release version taken once daily.

I've been meaning to ask about this — what is furosemide? I take it for swelling in my legs.

Furosemide is a diuretic, sometimes called a water pill. It helps your body get rid of extra fluid through urination. Because of this, it's usually best to take it in the morning so you're not up all night going to the bathroom. It can lower potassium levels, so your doctor may monitor that with blood tests, and sometimes they recommend eating potassium-rich foods like bananas or potatoes.

How do I know if I'm getting enough protein? I don't eat much meat anymore.

Protein needs actually increase somewhat with age because the body becomes less efficient at using protein to maintain muscle. The general recommendation for older adults is about 1.0-1.2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight per day — for a 70 kg person, that's roughly 70-85 grams.

You can absolutely meet your protein needs without much meat. Here are excellent non-meat sources:
• Eggs — about 6 grams of protein each, and they contain all essential amino acids.
• Greek yogurt — roughly 15-20 grams per cup, plus calcium for bones.
• Cottage cheese — about 14 grams per half-cup.
• Beans, lentils, and chickpeas — about 7-9 grams per half-cup cooked, plus lots of fiber.
• Tofu and tempeh — 10-15 grams per serving.
• Fish, if you eat it — a small fillet of salmon or cod gives 20-25 grams plus omega-3s.
• Nuts and seeds — a handful gives about 5-7 grams (but they're calorie-dense, so moderate).

Signs you might not be getting enough protein: wounds healing slowly, feeling weaker than expected, losing muscle, hair thinning more than usual.

A simple approach is to include a protein source at each meal — an egg at breakfast, beans or fish at lunch, yogurt as a snack, and some tofu or lentils at dinner. Spreading it throughout the day is more effective than getting it all at once.

Does it matter what time of day I take my statin?

It can matter, depending on which statin. Atorvastatin and rosuvastatin have longer half-lives, so they can be taken any time of day. But shorter-acting statins like simvastatin work best when taken in the evening, because your body produces most of its cholesterol overnight. The most important thing is to take it consistently, whatever time you choose.

I want to start exercising but I'm afraid of falling. What's the safest way to begin?

That's a really valid concern — fear of falling is one of the biggest barriers to staying active for many older adults. But here's the thing: appropriate exercise actually reduces your fall risk by improving strength, balance, and coordination.

The safest starting points:
• Chair-based exercises — seated marches, leg lifts, and arm movements. You can find free videos on YouTube specifically for seniors. These build strength with zero fall risk.
• Walking with support — start in your own home or hallway, where you can hold onto walls or furniture. Even 5 minutes of walking back and forth counts.
• Using a stationary bike — no balance required, and you can go as slowly as you want.
• Balance exercises near a wall or sturdy chair — standing on one foot for a few seconds while holding onto something, then building up.

Consider asking your doctor for a physical therapy referral. A PT can assess your specific balance issues, give you a tailored exercise program, and teach you how to get up from the floor safely — which ironically reduces the fear of falling because you know what to do if it happens.

Start with just 5-10 minutes and see how you feel. The goal isn't to become an athlete — it's to maintain the strength and balance to do the things you enjoy.

My neighbor mentioned something and I got curious — what's the safest pain reliever for someone over 65?

Acetaminophen is often considered the first choice for older adults because it has fewer stomach and kidney effects compared to NSAIDs like ibuprofen or naproxen. But the dose needs to be lower — usually no more than 3000mg per day. The safest option really depends on your individual health — your kidney function, liver health, and other medications all play a role.

I've been thinking about this — how can I lower my blood pressure naturally? I don't want to take more pills.

There are several things that can genuinely help lower blood pressure without medication — though for many people, these work best alongside medication rather than replacing it. Here are the most effective approaches:

Reduce salt (sodium) — this is probably the single biggest lever you can pull:
• Stop adding salt at the table and use less in cooking. Herbs, garlic, lemon juice, and spices add flavor instead.
• Watch for hidden salt in bread, canned soups, processed meats, and even breakfast cereals.
• Aim for under 1500-2000 mg of sodium per day if you can.

Eat more potassium-rich foods — potassium helps balance sodium:
• Bananas, potatoes, spinach, tomatoes, beans, and yogurt are all good sources.
• But check with your doctor first — some blood pressure medications (ACE inhibitors, ARBs) already raise potassium levels.

Regular movement makes a real difference:
• 30 minutes of walking most days can lower systolic pressure by 4-8 points.

Other things that help: maintaining a healthy weight, cutting back on alcohol, managing stress through deep breathing or meditation, and getting enough sleep.

The DASH diet (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) combines all of these principles. Your doctor may be able to refer you to a dietitian who can help tailor it to your preferences.

what are the side effects of valsartan

What are the possible side effects of valsartan tablets?  

Valsartan tablets may cause the following serious side effects:  

Injury or death to an unborn baby. See “What is the most important information I should know about valsartan tablets?”  

Low Blood Pressure (Hypotension). Low blood pressure is most likely to happen if you also take water pills, are on a low-salt diet, get dialysis treatments, have heart problems, or get sick with vomiting or diarrhea. Lie down, if you feel faint or dizzy. Call your doctor right away.   

Kidney problems. Kidney problems may get worse if you already have kidney disease. Some patients will have changes on blood tests for kidney function and may need a lower dose of valsartan tablets. Call your doctor if you get swelling in your feet, ankles, or hands, or unexplained weight gain. If you have heart failure, your doctor should check your kidney function before prescribing valsartan tablets.   

The most common side effects of valsartan tablets used to treat people with high blood pressure include:

headache

dizziness

flu symptoms

tiredness

stomach (abdominal) pain

Side effects were generally mild and brief. They generally have not caused patients to stop taking valsartan tablets.   

The most common side effects of valsartan tablets used to treat people with heart failure include:

dizziness
low blood pressure
diarrhea
joint and back pain
tiredness
high blood potassium

I've been meaning to ask about this — what's the difference between warfarin and the newer blood thinners like apixaban?

The main differences are convenience and monitoring. Warfarin requires regular blood tests to check your INR levels, and you need to be mindful of vitamin K in your diet. Newer medications like apixaban (Eliquis) don't need routine blood testing and have fewer food interactions. The newer ones also tend to wear off faster, which can be an advantage if you need surgery. However, they're more expensive and may not be right for everyone.

I read something and wanted to ask — i live alone and cooking for one feels like too much effort. I end up eating toast or nothing.

This is such a common challenge, and it's completely understandable — the motivation to cook often comes from sharing the meal with someone. But your body still needs good fuel even when you're eating solo.

Some ideas that might make it feel less burdensome:
• Cook a proper meal once or twice a week and portion out leftovers. A pot of soup, a casserole, or roasted vegetables and chicken can provide several meals with one effort.
• Batch-prep components rather than full meals — cook a pot of quinoa or brown rice, roast a tray of vegetables, and grill some chicken. Then mix and match through the week.
• Frozen vegetables are just as nutritious as fresh and don't go bad. You can take out exactly what you need.
• Keep some easy, nutritious fallbacks: eggs (quick protein), canned beans (fiber and protein), Greek yogurt, pre-washed salad greens.
• Sometimes just making the table setting nice — a placemat, a napkin, some music — can make eating alone feel more like a proper meal and less like an afterthought.

Toast isn't the worst thing in the world, but adding an egg on top or some avocado turns it into something that will sustain you much longer. You deserve to eat well, even when it's just you.

what should my blood sugar be before breakfast? I'm never sure if my numbers are okay.

For most older adults with diabetes, a reasonable fasting target (before breakfast) is around 4.4 to 7.2 mmol/L (80 to 130 mg/dL). But targets vary from person to person based on your age, how long you've had diabetes, other health conditions, and your risk of low blood sugar.

Your doctor may have set a different target for you specifically. Some doctors prefer slightly higher targets for older adults to reduce the risk of dangerous low blood sugar episodes.

Generally:
• Below 4.0 mmol/L (72 mg/dL) — too low, treat with fast-acting sugar like juice or glucose tablets.
• 4.4 – 7.2 mmol/L (80–130 mg/dL) — reasonable fasting range for most.
• Above 10.0 mmol/L (180 mg/dL) consistently — worth discussing with your doctor.

The best thing you can do is ask at your next appointment: 'What's the fasting range you want me to aim for?' Having a clear target from your own doctor will give you much more confidence when you check your numbers.

I've been thinking about this — are there any natural remedies for chronic pain that actually work?

Some natural approaches do have evidence behind them for chronic pain management. They're generally not as potent as medications for acute relief, but they can be effective as part of a broader management plan:

• Turmeric/curcumin — has anti-inflammatory properties. The challenge is absorption; products with black pepper extract (piperine) are better absorbed. It can interact with blood thinners, so check with your pharmacist if you're on warfarin or similar.
• Omega-3 fatty acids (fish oil) — have anti-inflammatory effects and there's reasonable evidence for helping with rheumatoid arthritis pain specifically.
• Ginger — also has anti-inflammatory compounds and some people find it helpful for osteoarthritis pain.
• Capsaicin cream — made from chili peppers, applied to the skin over a painful joint. It depletes substance P, a pain-signaling chemical. It causes a burning sensation initially but can provide significant relief after a week or two of consistent use.
• Mindfulness meditation and gentle yoga — strong evidence for reducing the suffering component of chronic pain, even when the pain sensation itself doesn't change.

Important: 'natural' doesn't automatically mean safe, especially when combined with prescription medications. Always tell your doctor and pharmacist about any supplements you're taking or considering.

I've been wondering — is it true that bitter melon can help lower blood sugar? My neighbor swears by it.

Bitter melon does contain compounds that may help lower blood sugar — there's actually some research behind it. It has substances that work a bit like insulin in the body. But there are a few important cautions:

• Bitter melon can interact with diabetes medications, especially insulin and sulfonylureas like glipizide, potentially causing blood sugar to drop too low.
• The effect varies a lot from person to person, so it's not something you can rely on as a replacement for your prescribed treatment.
• It can cause digestive upset in some people.

If you want to try it, please talk to your doctor or pharmacist first — they need to know because they may want to monitor your blood sugar more closely or adjust your medication dose. Think of it as something that might complement your current plan, not replace it.

My neighbor mentioned something and I got curious — why can't I just stop taking my blood pressure medicine if I feel fine?

Feeling fine on the medicine is usually a sign that it's working well — not that you don't need it. Stopping suddenly can cause your blood pressure to rebound, sometimes going even higher than before, which can put stress on your heart and arteries. If you ever want to stop or change a medication, it's safest to work with your doctor to taper it down gradually.

Some people find it helpful to use it just on the worst days, and use gentler approaches — like warm baths or topical creams — on other days. It might be worth asking your doctor about safer long-term options for managing arthritis pain.

I saw something online and wanted to check — my medication says 'extended release' on the bottle. What does that mean?

Extended release means the pill is designed to release the medicine slowly over many hours rather than all at once. This keeps a steady level in your body throughout the day and usually means you can take it less often. The important thing is that extended release tablets should never be crushed, chewed, or split unless your doctor specifically says it's okay — doing so can release too much medicine at once.

My neighbor mentioned something and I got curious — what's the difference between ibuprofen and naproxen?

Both are NSAIDs that help with pain and inflammation, but the main difference is how long they last. Ibuprofen works for about 4-6 hours, while naproxen lasts longer — around 8-12 hours. That means naproxen is often taken just twice a day. Both can affect the stomach and kidneys, so like other NSAIDs, they're best used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest time needed.

why would my urine test be negative for benzodiazepines when i take ativan

Common limitations exist for screening benzodiazepines when using traditional immunoassay (IA) tests. IA testing for benzodiazepines often targets nordiazepam and oxazepam to measure whether an antibody-antigen response occurs, resulting in a positive or negative test result. Other benzodiazepine compounds are tested for their ability to cross react with the target drug in an IA technique. In other words, low cross-reactivity of other drugs can result in false negatives for the other benzodiazepines. || Some commonly prescribed drugs have limited cross-reactivity. For example, lorazepam and 7-aminoclonazepam, the primary metabolite of clonazepam, have limited cross-reactivity with traditional IAs due to their molecular structures. Therefore, it is not uncommon for a laboratory to obtain a “negative” IA result for lorazepam and 7-aminoclonazepam that is indeed positive once confirmation testing with a mass spectrometer (MS) is performed.

What is furosemide? I take it for swelling in my legs.

Furosemide is a diuretic, sometimes called a water pill. It helps your body get rid of extra fluid through urination. Because of this, it's usually best to take it in the morning so you're not up all night going to the bathroom. It can lower potassium levels, so your doctor may monitor that with blood tests, and sometimes they recommend eating potassium-rich foods like bananas or potatoes.

Do any of my medications mean I shouldn't get the flu shot?

For the standard flu shot, almost no medications are a reason not to get it. In fact, many conditions that require regular medication — like diabetes, heart disease, or COPD — are exactly the reasons the flu shot is especially recommended. The only people who need to be more cautious are those with severe egg allergies or those on very high-dose immunosuppressive medications.

I've been thinking about this — how do I know if I'm getting enough protein? I don't eat much meat anymore.

Protein needs actually increase somewhat with age because the body becomes less efficient at using protein to maintain muscle. The general recommendation for older adults is about 1.0-1.2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight per day — for a 70 kg person, that's roughly 70-85 grams.

You can absolutely meet your protein needs without much meat. Here are excellent non-meat sources:
• Eggs — about 6 grams of protein each, and they contain all essential amino acids.
• Greek yogurt — roughly 15-20 grams per cup, plus calcium for bones.
• Cottage cheese — about 14 grams per half-cup.
• Beans, lentils, and chickpeas — about 7-9 grams per half-cup cooked, plus lots of fiber.
• Tofu and tempeh — 10-15 grams per serving.
• Fish, if you eat it — a small fillet of salmon or cod gives 20-25 grams plus omega-3s.
• Nuts and seeds — a handful gives about 5-7 grams (but they're calorie-dense, so moderate).

Signs you might not be getting enough protein: wounds healing slowly, feeling weaker than expected, losing muscle, hair thinning more than usual.

A simple approach is to include a protein source at each meal — an egg at breakfast, beans or fish at lunch, yogurt as a snack, and some tofu or lentils at dinner. Spreading it throughout the day is more effective than getting it all at once.

I take levothyroxine every morning on an empty stomach. Why is that important?

Levothyroxine replaces the thyroid hormone your body isn't making enough of. Taking it on an empty stomach is important because food, especially calcium-rich foods and fiber, can reduce how well your body absorbs it. Waiting 30-60 minutes before eating helps ensure you get the full dose. Also, it's best to take it at least 4 hours apart from calcium or iron supplements.

What should my blood sugar be before breakfast? I'm never sure if my numbers are okay.

For most older adults with diabetes, a reasonable fasting target (before breakfast) is around 4.4 to 7.2 mmol/L (80 to 130 mg/dL). But targets vary from person to person based on your age, how long you've had diabetes, other health conditions, and your risk of low blood sugar.

Your doctor may have set a different target for you specifically. Some doctors prefer slightly higher targets for older adults to reduce the risk of dangerous low blood sugar episodes.

Generally:
• Below 4.0 mmol/L (72 mg/dL) — too low, treat with fast-acting sugar like juice or glucose tablets.
• 4.4 – 7.2 mmol/L (80–130 mg/dL) — reasonable fasting range for most.
• Above 10.0 mmol/L (180 mg/dL) consistently — worth discussing with your doctor.

The best thing you can do is ask at your next appointment: 'What's the fasting range you want me to aim for?' Having a clear target from your own doctor will give you much more confidence when you check your numbers.

what antidepressants are best for someone with a heart arrhythmia

A research team at Harvard-affiliated Massachusetts General Hospital has verified that several antidepressants may increase the risk of a potentially dangerous heart rhythm disturbance (arrhythmia). By examining the electrocardiograms (ECGs) of more than 38,000 people taking 11 different antidepressants, they confirmed that higher doses of citalopram (Celexa), amitriptyline (Elavil), and escitalopram (Lexapro) were associated with a slight disruption of the electrical function of the heart. This delay as seen on an ECG is known as a prolonged Q-T interval, and it is a risk factor for an arrhythmia that causes sudden death. Although the majority of people with this ECG abnormality never develop an arrhythmia, the researchers cautioned that people taking these antidepressants should discuss the risk with their doctor. The researchers emphasized that no one with a history of arrhythmias should start taking these medications. Instead, such a patient might try one of the antidepressants that do not increase the Q-T interval: fluoxetine (Prozac), paroxetine (Paxil), sertraline (Zoloft), or buproprion (Wellbutrin). The study was published Jan. 29, 2013, in the journal BMJ.

I've been meaning to ask about this — why do some medicines need to be taken with food and others on an empty stomach?

It comes down to how the food affects the medicine. Some medicines can irritate the stomach lining, so food acts as a buffer — ibuprofen is a good example of this. Others, like certain antibiotics or thyroid medication, have their absorption blocked by food or specific minerals like calcium, so they work best on an empty stomach. The label instructions are there to help the medicine work as intended.

Quick question — what is amlodipine used for?

Amlodipine is a calcium channel blocker that helps relax and widen your blood vessels, which lowers blood pressure. It's taken once a day. Some people notice mild ankle swelling when taking it — this is usually not dangerous, but if it bothers you or the swelling is significant, it's worth mentioning to your doctor.

Is it safe to take ibuprofen every day for arthritis? My knees have been really bothering me.

Ibuprofen can help with arthritis pain, but taking it every day for a long time isn't usually recommended because it can affect the stomach lining and kidneys over time. Some people find it helpful to use it just on the worst days, and use gentler approaches — like warm baths or topical creams — on other days. It might be worth asking your doctor about safer long-term options for managing arthritis pain.

heroin mixed with fentanyl what are the symptoms of an overdose

Acute overdose with Fentanyl Citrate Injection can be manifested by respiratory depression, somnolence progressing to stupor or coma, skeletal muscle flaccidity, cold and clammy skin, constricted pupils, and, in some cases, pulmonary edema, bradycardia, hypotension, partial or complete airway obstruction, atypical snoring, and death.

how much iron should an anemic take per day

The amount of iron needed to treat patients with iron deficiency is higher than the amount found in most daily multivitamin supplements. The amount of iron prescribed by your doctor will be in milligrams (mg) of elemental iron. Most people with iron deficiency need 150-200 mg per day of elemental iron (2 to 5 mg of iron per kilogram of body weight per day). Ask your doctor how many milligrams of iron you should be taking per day. If you take vitamins, bring them to your doctor's visit to be sure.

what medications can you not take with grapefruit

Here are examples of some types of drugs that grapefruit juice can cause problems with (interact):

Some statin drugs to lower cholesterol, such as Zocor (simvastatin) and Lipitor (atorvastatin).
Some drugs that treat high blood pressure, such as Procardia and Adalat CC (both nifedipine).
Some organ-transplant rejection drugs, such as Sandimmune and Neoral (both cyclosporine).
Some anti-anxiety drugs, such as buspirone.
Some corticosteroids that treat Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis, such as Entocort EC and Uceris (both budesonide).
Some drugs that treat abnormal heart rhythms, such as Pacerone and Nexterone (both amiodarone).
Some antihistamines, such as Allegra (fexofenadine).

I take so many pills, I'm starting to lose track. Any tips for staying organized?

That's a really common challenge, and it's smart to look for solutions before anything gets missed. A weekly pill organizer is the simplest tool — you can fill it once a week and easily see if you've taken each dose. Some pharmacies also offer blister packaging where they sort your pills by day and time. Your pharmacist can often help you work out a practical system.

What is ibuprofen? My neighbor gave me one for my headache.

Ibuprofen is a common pain reliever that helps with headaches, muscle aches, and inflammation. It belongs to a group of medicines called NSAIDs. One thing to keep in mind is that it can sometimes upset the stomach if taken on an empty stomach, so having it with food helps. If you take other medications or have any kidney issues, it might be worth checking with a pharmacist before taking it regularly.

Quick question — my metformin makes my stomach upset. Is there anything I can do?

Stomach upset is one of the most common complaints when starting metformin, but there are a few things that can help. Taking it with meals or right after eating makes a big difference. Starting with a lower dose and going up slowly can also help your body adjust. If you're on the regular version, the extended-release form often causes less stomach trouble.
