I've been thinking about this — what should my blood sugar be before breakfast? I'm never sure if my numbers are okay.

For most older adults with diabetes, a reasonable fasting target (before breakfast) is around 4.4 to 7.2 mmol/L (80 to 130 mg/dL). But targets vary from person to person based on your age, how long you've had diabetes, other health conditions, and your risk of low blood sugar.

Your doctor may have set a different target for you specifically. Some doctors prefer slightly higher targets for older adults to reduce the risk of dangerous low blood sugar episodes.

Generally:
• Below 4.0 mmol/L (72 mg/dL) — too low, treat with fast-acting sugar like juice or glucose tablets.
• 4.4 – 7.2 mmol/L (80–130 mg/dL) — reasonable fasting range for most.
• Above 10.0 mmol/L (180 mg/dL) consistently — worth discussing with your doctor.

The best thing you can do is ask at your next appointment: 'What's the fasting range you want me to aim for?' Having a clear target from your own doctor will give you much more confidence when you check your numbers.

My ankles have been swelling since I started amlodipine. Is that dangerous?

Ankle swelling is actually one of the most common side effects of amlodipine, and for most people it's not dangerous — just uncomfortable. It happens because the medication widens your blood vessels, and a little fluid can leak into the surrounding tissues. Elevating your feet when sitting and reducing salt intake can help.

what is amlodipine ?

Amlodipine is used alone or in combination with other medications to treat high blood pressure and chest pain (angina). Amlodipine is in a class of medications called calcium channel blockers. It lowers blood pressure by relaxing the blood vessels so the heart does not have to pump as hard. It controls chest pain by increasing the supply of blood to the heart. If taken regularly, amlodipine controls chest pain, but it does not stop chest pain once it starts. Your doctor may prescribe a different medication to take when you have chest pain.

what does metformin do

Metformin helps to control the amount of glucose (sugar) in your blood. It decreases the amount of glucose you absorb from your food and the amount of glucose made by your liver. Metformin also increases your body's response to insulin, a natural substance that controls the amount of glucose in the blood.

I've been wondering — what kind of exercise is best for lowering blood sugar? I'm 68 and not very fit.

At 68, the best exercise is the one you'll actually do and enjoy — consistency matters much more than intensity for blood sugar management.

Walking is honestly one of the most effective options. Just 15-20 minutes after meals can make a noticeable difference in your post-meal blood sugar because your muscles use glucose directly from your bloodstream when they're active.
• Start with 10-15 minutes of walking after lunch or dinner, and build up gradually.
• Chair exercises are great if standing for long periods is hard — seated leg lifts, arm circles, and gentle marching in place all count.
• Tai chi and gentle yoga improve both physical fitness and balance, and many community centers offer classes specifically for seniors.
• Strength matters too — even light hand weights or resistance bands twice a week help your muscles become more responsive to insulin.

The most important thing is to start where you are. If 10 minutes feels like enough, that's your starting point. Check with your doctor before starting anything new, especially if you have heart concerns, but for most people, gentle daily movement is both safe and beneficial.

I saw something online and wanted to check — my ankles have been swelling since I started amlodipine. Is that dangerous?

Ankle swelling is actually one of the most common side effects of amlodipine, and for most people it's not dangerous — just uncomfortable. It happens because the medication widens your blood vessels, and a little fluid can leak into the surrounding tissues. Elevating your feet when sitting and reducing salt intake can help.

what is bioflavonoid

Bioflavonoids are a group of what are called “polyphenolic” plant-derived compounds. They’re also called flavonoids. There are between 4,000 and 6,000 different varieties known. Some are used in medicine, supplements, or for other health purposes.

I've been thinking about this — i take so many supplements but I'm not sure any of them actually help. How do I know?

You're asking a really good question, and honestly, many people are in the same boat — taking supplements out of habit without knowing if they're making a difference.

A few things to consider:
• Supplements are not regulated the same way medications are. The quality and actual content can vary a lot between brands.
• Some supplements can interact with prescription medications in significant ways. St. John's Wort, for example, interferes with many drugs. Even 'natural' doesn't mean harmless.
• Vitamin D and B12 are two supplements that actually have good evidence for many older adults, and your doctor can check your blood levels to know if you truly need them. Calcium may be helpful for bone health but recent research has raised questions about supplementing high doses.

The best approach is to bring all your supplements — bottles and all — to your next doctor's appointment and go through them one by one. Ask: 'Is there evidence this helps someone in my situation? Could any of these interact with my medications? Are there any I can stop taking?'

A good pharmacist can also review your supplements alongside your medications. You might find you can simplify your routine and save money without losing any real benefit.

Downward dose adjustment of these drugs may be required when given concomitantly with methylphenidate. Do I really need to take my blood pressure medicine if my blood pressure was normal at the last checkup?

That's a really common question. If your blood pressure was normal at the checkup, it's likely because the medicine is working — not because you don't need it anymore. Blood pressure medications don't cure high blood pressure; they manage it. Stopping suddenly can cause a rebound effect where your pressure goes even higher than before.

I read something and wanted to ask — i have constant low back pain. I've tried painkillers but I don't want to rely on them. What else can I do?

Chronic back pain is exhausting, and wanting to manage it without constant painkillers makes total sense — long-term use of NSAIDs and even acetaminophen comes with risks.

The most effective non-drug approaches, based on research:
• Movement therapy — physical therapy, clinical Pilates, or McKenzie method exercises. A good PT can identify the specific cause of your back pain and give you targeted exercises. The key word is targeted — random YouTube exercises might help or might make things worse.
• Heat therapy — a heating pad or warm bath can relax tight muscles and provide genuine relief, especially first thing in the morning.
• Strengthening the core and glutes — weak abdominal and hip muscles mean your back has to do more work. Gentle bridging exercises and pelvic tilts are good starting points. A PT can guide you.
• Walking — it sounds counterintuitive when your back hurts, but gentle walking keeps the spine mobile and the muscles from seizing up.
• Improving sleep posture — a pillow under your knees if you sleep on your back, or between your knees if you sleep on your side, can reduce spinal strain overnight.

Some people also find relief through acupuncture, massage therapy, or mindfulness-based stress reduction (pain and stress reinforce each other).

Red flags that need medical attention: pain that goes down your leg below the knee, numbness in your groin area, loss of bladder or bowel control, or pain that wakes you from sleep. If any of these develop, see a doctor promptly.

what is the difference between gabbentibe lyrica "?" for pain

Though pregabalin and gabapentin have somewhat similar pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic profiles, there are clearly significant differences. Overall, pregabalin has more predictable pharmacokinetics, and it also shows a stronger binding affinity to its target receptor, increased potency, and a steeper dose-response curve in neuropathic pain that does not plateau over recommended dosing levels. A few studies have found that pregabalin has fewer side effects and may be more efficacious for neuropathic pain than gabapentin. Several studies reviewing conversion of gabapentin to pregabalin predict that a rough ratio for conversion is about 6:1 gabapentin to pregabalin. In addition, a direct switch from gabapentin to pregabalin seems to be well tolerated, making the conversion simple. Clinicians should note that pregabalin has various pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic advantages over gabapentin, and a conversion between the 2 medications is often well tolerated.

Does weather really affect arthritis? My knees seem to know when rain is coming.

Many people with arthritis report this, and there's actually some science behind it. Changes in barometric pressure (the weight of the air around us) can cause tissues and joint fluid to expand or contract slightly, which may affect already-sensitive joints.

Cold weather can also make the fluid inside joints thicker and muscles tighter, which adds to stiffness. Humidity seems to play a role too.

What can help on weather-change days:
• Keep the affected joints warm — compression sleeves or warm clothing can help.
• Gentle movement throughout the day prevents joints from stiffening up.
• A warm bath or shower when you first notice the ache can head off worse pain.
• Staying well-hydrated helps keep joint fluid at a better consistency.

It's not just in your head — you're not imagining it. Your joints are picking up on changes that the rest of you might not even notice.

Quick question — what kind of exercise is best for lowering blood sugar? I'm 68 and not very fit.

At 68, the best exercise is the one you'll actually do and enjoy — consistency matters much more than intensity for blood sugar management.

Walking is honestly one of the most effective options. Just 15-20 minutes after meals can make a noticeable difference in your post-meal blood sugar because your muscles use glucose directly from your bloodstream when they're active.
• Start with 10-15 minutes of walking after lunch or dinner, and build up gradually.
• Chair exercises are great if standing for long periods is hard — seated leg lifts, arm circles, and gentle marching in place all count.
• Tai chi and gentle yoga improve both physical fitness and balance, and many community centers offer classes specifically for seniors.
• Strength matters too — even light hand weights or resistance bands twice a week help your muscles become more responsive to insulin.

The most important thing is to start where you are. If 10 minutes feels like enough, that's your starting point. Check with your doctor before starting anything new, especially if you have heart concerns, but for most people, gentle daily movement is both safe and beneficial.

What checkups should someone my age be getting? I feel like I'm missing things.

It's smart to be proactive about this. Recommendations vary based on your personal and family history, but here are the general screenings most guidelines recommend for adults over 65:

Annual:
• Blood pressure check — at least once a year, more often if you have hypertension.
• Annual wellness visit with your primary care doctor — a chance to review medications, discuss concerns, and update preventive screenings.
• Flu shot — every fall/winter. Older adults are at higher risk of flu complications.

Regular intervals:
• Blood work — usually annually, including cholesterol panel, kidney function, liver function, and blood sugar (HbA1c).
• Colon cancer screening — colonoscopy every 10 years, or stool-based tests more frequently, up to age 75. After 75, discuss with your doctor whether screening still makes sense.
• Mammogram (women) — every 1-2 years, depending on risk factors, up to about age 75.
• Bone density scan (DEXA) — at least once for women over 65 and men over 70.
• Diabetes screening — every 3 years, or more often if you have risk factors.
• Vision and hearing checks — annually.
• Dental cleanings — every 6 months.

Once (or as recommended):
• Pneumonia vaccine — usually two different types, given a year apart.
• Shingles vaccine — two-dose series for adults over 50.
• Tdap booster — if you haven't had one in the last 10 years.

The best approach is to ask your doctor directly at your next visit: 'What screenings am I due for?' They can print you a personalized schedule.

Quick question — should I avoid leafy greens if I take warfarin?

You don't need to avoid them completely — in fact, leafy greens are very healthy. The key with warfarin is consistency, not elimination. Leafy greens like spinach, kale, and broccoli are high in vitamin K, which can reduce warfarin's effectiveness. But if you eat roughly the same amount each week, your warfarin dose can be adjusted to match.

I've been thinking about this — my cholesterol is a little high but my doctor hasn't put me on medication. Should I be worried?

If your doctor isn't prescribing medication yet, it likely means your overall risk isn't in the range where medication clearly provides more benefit than risk. Doctors look at cholesterol numbers in the context of everything else — your age, blood pressure, whether you smoke, and whether you have diabetes.

A 'little high' LDL might be something your doctor believes can be improved with diet and exercise first, which is a completely reasonable approach. Lifestyle changes can lower LDL by 10-20% for many people.

That said, it's absolutely fair to ask your doctor directly: 'Based on my numbers and overall health, what's my risk of a heart attack or stroke in the next 10 years?' This is called a cardiovascular risk assessment, and it's a standard calculation doctors can do. The answer will tell you how aggressively you need to manage your cholesterol.

In the meantime, focusing on the dietary changes we discussed — more soluble fiber, healthy fats instead of saturated fats, and regular walking — is a good foundation regardless of whether you ever need medication.

Hey, can I ask you something? how do I improve my balance? I've noticed I'm a bit wobbly lately.

Balance naturally declines with age, but it's also very trainable — you can improve it at any age with consistent practice.

Simple exercises to do daily (near a wall or sturdy chair for support):
• Single-leg stand — hold onto a chair and lift one foot slightly off the ground. Start with 5-10 seconds and build up. Do this on each side.
• Heel-to-toe walking — walk in a straight line placing the heel of one foot directly in front of the toes of the other, like walking on a tightrope.
• Sit-to-stand — practice standing up from a chair without using your hands. This builds both leg strength and balance. Start with 5 repetitions.
• Side leg raises — stand behind a chair, hold on, and lift one leg out to the side. This strengthens hip muscles, which are important for stability.

Tai chi is one of the best-studied activities for improving balance in older adults — many community centers offer beginner classes.

Other things that affect balance: vision (when was your last eye check?), footwear (supportive, non-slip shoes help), and some medications (certain blood pressure drugs or sedatives can cause dizziness). If the wobbliness is new or getting worse, mention it to your doctor — there may be an underlying cause they can address.

Is it okay to exercise if I have high blood pressure? I'm worried I'll make it worse.

Exercise is actually one of the best things you can do for high blood pressure — it strengthens your heart so it can pump blood with less effort, which reduces the force on your arteries. Regular aerobic exercise can lower your systolic pressure by 5-8 points.

The key is starting gently and being consistent:
• Walking, swimming, and cycling are excellent choices — low-impact but effective.
• Start with 15-20 minutes and build up slowly over weeks, not days.
• Avoid holding your breath when lifting anything — this can cause a temporary blood pressure spike. Exhale during the effort.
• If you get very short of breath, feel dizzy, or have chest pain, stop and rest. These are signs to ease off.

Isometric exercises (like heavy weightlifting or plank holds) can temporarily spike blood pressure more than walking or swimming, so those are worth being more cautious with.

As always, check with your doctor before starting, but for the vast majority of people with hypertension, regular moderate exercise is not just safe — it's recommended.

I've been meaning to ask about this — my pharmacist gave me the generic version instead of the brand name. Is it just as good?

Generic medications contain the same active ingredient at the same dose as the brand name, and they go through rigorous testing to prove they're equivalent. The difference is in the inactive ingredients — the fillers, binders, and dyes — which can make the pill look different. For the vast majority of medications, generics work just as well and cost significantly less.

Hey, can I ask you something? i have constant low back pain. I've tried painkillers but I don't want to rely on them. What else can I do?

Chronic back pain is exhausting, and wanting to manage it without constant painkillers makes total sense — long-term use of NSAIDs and even acetaminophen comes with risks.

The most effective non-drug approaches, based on research:
• Movement therapy — physical therapy, clinical Pilates, or McKenzie method exercises. A good PT can identify the specific cause of your back pain and give you targeted exercises. The key word is targeted — random YouTube exercises might help or might make things worse.
• Heat therapy — a heating pad or warm bath can relax tight muscles and provide genuine relief, especially first thing in the morning.
• Strengthening the core and glutes — weak abdominal and hip muscles mean your back has to do more work. Gentle bridging exercises and pelvic tilts are good starting points. A PT can guide you.
• Walking — it sounds counterintuitive when your back hurts, but gentle walking keeps the spine mobile and the muscles from seizing up.
• Improving sleep posture — a pillow under your knees if you sleep on your back, or between your knees if you sleep on your side, can reduce spinal strain overnight.

Some people also find relief through acupuncture, massage therapy, or mindfulness-based stress reduction (pain and stress reinforce each other).

Red flags that need medical attention: pain that goes down your leg below the knee, numbness in your groin area, loss of bladder or bowel control, or pain that wakes you from sleep. If any of these develop, see a doctor promptly.

Quick question — what does 'off-label' mean? My doctor prescribed something and said it's off-label.

Off-label means a medication is being used for a purpose that wasn't in its original FDA approval. This is actually quite common and perfectly legal. For example, gabapentin was approved for seizures but is often prescribed for nerve pain. It doesn't mean the use is unsafe — there's usually research supporting it — but the manufacturer just didn't go through the formal approval process for that particular use.

Can I take ibuprofen if I'm on warfarin?

This is an important combination to be careful with. Ibuprofen and other NSAIDs can increase the risk of bleeding when taken with warfarin, because both affect how the blood clots. Many doctors recommend avoiding NSAIDs altogether when you're on warfarin. Acetaminophen is often suggested as a safer alternative for pain, but even that should be discussed with your doctor if you're on warfarin.

I've been thinking about this — my blood pressure is always high at the doctor's office but fine at home. Which one should I trust?

This is incredibly common — it's called white coat hypertension, and it affects a lot of people. The stress of being in a medical setting temporarily raises your pressure.

Generally, your home readings are more reliable for understanding your true day-to-day blood pressure. The doctor is usually more interested in your home numbers for making treatment decisions.

Some tips for getting accurate home readings:
• Sit quietly for 5 minutes before measuring, with your feet flat on the floor and your back supported.
• Don't measure right after exercise, coffee, or smoking.
• Take readings at the same times each day — morning and evening are standard.
• Take 2-3 readings, about a minute apart, and record the average.

Bring your home monitor to your next appointment so they can check that it's calibrated correctly. And if your home readings are consistently normal, that's genuinely reassuring.

pneumococcal vaccine how often

CDC recommends routine administration of pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13 or Prevnar13®) for all children younger than 2 years of age:

Give PCV13 to infants as a series of 4 doses, one dose at each of these ages: 2 months, 4 months, 6 months, and 12 through 15 months.
Children who miss their shots or start the series later should still get the vaccine. The number of doses recommended and the intervals between doses will depend on the child’s age when vaccination begins.
Immunization Schedules

 HCP adult and child schedules
View current schedules for children, teens, and adults.

CDC recommends pneumococcal vaccination (PCV13 or Prevnar13®, and PPSV23 or Pneumovax23®) for all adults 65 years or older:

Give a dose of PCV13 to adults 65 years or older who have not previously received a dose. Then administer a dose of PPSV23 at least 1 year later.
If the patient already received one or more doses of PPSV23, give the dose of PCV13 at least 1 year after they received the most recent dose of PPSV23.

What is clopidogrel? I take it along with aspirin.

Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet medication — it helps stop blood cells called platelets from sticking together and forming clots. Taking it with aspirin is called dual antiplatelet therapy, and it's often prescribed after someone has had a stent placed or a heart attack. The combination is usually taken for a specific period of time determined by your cardiologist.

how can I lower my blood pressure naturally? I don't want to take more pills.

There are several things that can genuinely help lower blood pressure without medication — though for many people, these work best alongside medication rather than replacing it. Here are the most effective approaches:

Reduce salt (sodium) — this is probably the single biggest lever you can pull:
• Stop adding salt at the table and use less in cooking. Herbs, garlic, lemon juice, and spices add flavor instead.
• Watch for hidden salt in bread, canned soups, processed meats, and even breakfast cereals.
• Aim for under 1500-2000 mg of sodium per day if you can.

Eat more potassium-rich foods — potassium helps balance sodium:
• Bananas, potatoes, spinach, tomatoes, beans, and yogurt are all good sources.
• But check with your doctor first — some blood pressure medications (ACE inhibitors, ARBs) already raise potassium levels.

Regular movement makes a real difference:
• 30 minutes of walking most days can lower systolic pressure by 4-8 points.

Other things that help: maintaining a healthy weight, cutting back on alcohol, managing stress through deep breathing or meditation, and getting enough sleep.

The DASH diet (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) combines all of these principles. Your doctor may be able to refer you to a dietitian who can help tailor it to your preferences.

how should I store my medications? The bathroom cabinet seems convenient.

The bathroom cabinet is actually not ideal for most medicines, because the heat and humidity from showers can break down the active ingredients over time. A cool, dry place like a bedroom drawer or kitchen cabinet away from the stove is better. Also, some medications need to be kept in the refrigerator, and others are sensitive to light. Keeping them in their original containers with the labels readable helps you stay organized too.

Hey, can I ask you something? my blood pressure is always high at the doctor's office but fine at home. Which one should I trust?

This is incredibly common — it's called white coat hypertension, and it affects a lot of people. The stress of being in a medical setting temporarily raises your pressure.

Generally, your home readings are more reliable for understanding your true day-to-day blood pressure. The doctor is usually more interested in your home numbers for making treatment decisions.

Some tips for getting accurate home readings:
• Sit quietly for 5 minutes before measuring, with your feet flat on the floor and your back supported.
• Don't measure right after exercise, coffee, or smoking.
• Take readings at the same times each day — morning and evening are standard.
• Take 2-3 readings, about a minute apart, and record the average.

Bring your home monitor to your next appointment so they can check that it's calibrated correctly. And if your home readings are consistently normal, that's genuinely reassuring.

Hey, can I ask you something? are there any natural remedies for chronic pain that actually work?

Some natural approaches do have evidence behind them for chronic pain management. They're generally not as potent as medications for acute relief, but they can be effective as part of a broader management plan:

• Turmeric/curcumin — has anti-inflammatory properties. The challenge is absorption; products with black pepper extract (piperine) are better absorbed. It can interact with blood thinners, so check with your pharmacist if you're on warfarin or similar.
• Omega-3 fatty acids (fish oil) — have anti-inflammatory effects and there's reasonable evidence for helping with rheumatoid arthritis pain specifically.
• Ginger — also has anti-inflammatory compounds and some people find it helpful for osteoarthritis pain.
• Capsaicin cream — made from chili peppers, applied to the skin over a painful joint. It depletes substance P, a pain-signaling chemical. It causes a burning sensation initially but can provide significant relief after a week or two of consistent use.
• Mindfulness meditation and gentle yoga — strong evidence for reducing the suffering component of chronic pain, even when the pain sensation itself doesn't change.

Important: 'natural' doesn't automatically mean safe, especially when combined with prescription medications. Always tell your doctor and pharmacist about any supplements you're taking or considering.

I read something and wanted to ask — my doctor said my blood pressure is 145 over 90. Is that really that bad at my age?

A reading of 145/90 is considered stage 1 hypertension. At any age, blood pressure consistently in this range puts extra strain on your heart, blood vessels, and kidneys over time. It increases the risk of stroke, heart attack, and kidney problems.

However, treatment decisions for older adults are more nuanced than just looking at a number. Your doctor considers your overall health, other conditions, risk of falls (if blood pressure drops too low from medication), and quality of life.

For many people in their 60s and 70s, a target of below 140/90 is reasonable, and for some, below 130/80 if tolerated well. But if you have issues with dizziness or falls, a slightly higher target may be safer.

The good news is that 145/90 is often very responsive to lifestyle changes — cutting back on salt, walking more, and losing even a modest amount of weight can bring it down meaningfully. Your doctor is flagging this now so you have a chance to address it before it causes problems.

Hey, can I ask you something? is it normal to need less sleep as you get older? I used to sleep 8 hours, now I'm lucky to get 6.

It's a common belief that older adults need less sleep, but the research actually shows that sleep needs stay about the same throughout adulthood — around 7-8 hours. What changes is the ability to get that sleep in one continuous block.

As we age, we spend less time in deep sleep and more time in lighter sleep stages, which means we wake up more easily. The body's internal clock also shifts earlier — you might feel sleepy earlier in the evening and wake up earlier in the morning. This is normal, but it doesn't mean you need less total sleep.

The 6 hours you're getting may or may not be enough — the real question is how you feel during the day. If you're tired, nodding off, or having trouble concentrating, you probably need more. If you feel rested and functional on 6 hours, that might genuinely be enough for you.

If you're feeling sleep-deprived, focus on sleep quality more than quantity — a dark, cool, quiet bedroom and a consistent bedtime routine make the sleep you do get more restorative.

My neighbor mentioned something and I got curious — what is omeprazole? I've been prescribed it for heartburn.

Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor — it reduces how much acid your stomach produces. It's used for heartburn, GERD, and to protect the stomach when taking certain pain medications. It works best when taken about 30-60 minutes before a meal, usually in the morning. Most people take it for a few weeks to a few months, and long-term use should be reviewed by a doctor periodically.

can I still eat fruit if I have diabetes? I love mangoes and bananas.

You can absolutely still enjoy fruit — having diabetes doesn't mean you have to give up the foods you love. The key is choosing wisely and managing portions.

Fruits like mangoes and bananas are on the sweeter, higher-carb side, but that doesn't mean they're off limits. Try these approaches:
• Have a smaller portion — half a banana or a few slices of mango instead of the whole fruit.
• Pair fruit with a bit of protein or healthy fat — a small handful of nuts or a spoonful of plain yogurt slows down how fast the sugar hits your bloodstream.
• Eat fruit as part of a meal rather than on an empty stomach.
• Timing matters — your body handles carbs better earlier in the day and after physical activity.

Lower-sugar fruits like berries, cherries, and green apples are a bit easier on blood sugar if you want something you can eat more of. But a little mango now and then, enjoyed mindfully, is part of a balanced life, not a failure.

my fasting blood sugar was 13 this morning. I'm really worried. What should I do?

That number is definitely on the high side, and I understand why you're concerned. With a fasting reading of 13 mmol/L (that's about 234 mg/dL), this is something you'll want your doctor to know about soon — don't wait for your next routine appointment.

In the meantime, here are some things that can help right now:
• Drink plenty of water — it helps your body flush out extra sugar through urine.
• Avoid anything sugary or high in carbs today — bread, rice, fruit juice, and sweets will push your numbers higher.
• A gentle walk after meals can help bring blood sugar down, but if you feel unwell or your numbers are very high, rest instead.
• Check your blood sugar again in a few hours to see which direction it's heading.

Watch for warning signs that need immediate medical attention: nausea, vomiting, fruity-smelling breath, or trouble breathing. If any of those appear, please go to the emergency room.

I know this feels scary, but one high reading doesn't mean everything is falling apart. Your doctor can help you figure out what caused the spike and adjust your plan. You're doing the right thing by paying attention to it.

what is divalproex

Divalproex sodium delayed-release capsules are indicated as monotherapy and adjunctive therapy in the treatment of adult patients and pediatric patients down to the age of 10 years with complex partial seizures that occur either in isolation or in association with other types of seizures. Divalproex sodium delayed-release capsules are also indicated for use as sole and adjunctive therapy in the treatment of simple and complex absence seizures, and adjunctively in patients with multiple seizure types that include absence seizures.

what are the side effects of insulin? I might need to start it.

Starting insulin can feel like a big step, but it's been used safely for many decades. The most common side effect is low blood sugar if the dose is higher than what your body needs — this can make you feel shaky, sweaty, or confused. Carrying a small snack or glucose tablets helps. Some people also notice a little redness at the injection site, which usually fades.

My neighbor mentioned something and I got curious — i heard I shouldn't eat bananas with my blood pressure medication. Is that true?

It depends on which blood pressure medication you're taking. If you're on an ACE inhibitor like lisinopril or an ARB like losartan, these medications can raise potassium levels slightly, and eating a lot of potassium-rich foods like bananas, potatoes, and oranges could push your levels too high. But for most people with healthy kidneys eating a normal diet, moderate banana consumption is fine.

drug therapyof gastritis when taking diclofenac sodium

When taken for a long time, painkillers like diclofenac and ibuprofen can lead to ulcers in the stomach and duodenum. But the risk of this happening can be clearly reduced by also taking medication to protect your stomach. || Various medications can lower the risk of getting a peptic ulcer. These include, in particular, drugs called proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) and H2 blockers. Both of these types of drugs reduce the production of stomach acid. In Germany, the most commonly used PPIs are omeprazole and pantoprazole. The H2 blocker that is normally used is ranitidine.

The hormone-like drug misoprostol is sometimes used too, although less often. One of the things misoprostol does is increase the production of gastric (stomach) mucus, which helps protect the stomach wall. In Germany, misoprostol is available as a combination drug together with the painkiller diclofenac.

what is insulin and glycogen

Insulin and glucagon work in what’s called a negative feedback loop. During this process, one event triggers another, which triggers another, and so on, to keep your blood sugar levels balanced.

How insulin works
During digestion, foods that contain carbohydrates are converted into glucose. Most of this glucose is sent into your bloodstream, causing a rise in blood glucose levels. This increase in blood glucose signals your pancreas to produce insulin.

The insulin tells cells throughout your body to take in glucose from your bloodstream. As the glucose moves into your cells, your blood glucose levels go down. Some cells use the glucose as energy. Other cells, such as in your liver and muscles, store any excess glucose as a substance called glycogen. Your body uses glycogen for fuel between meals.

||

How glucagon works
Glucagon works to counterbalance the actions of insulin.

About four to six hours after you eat, the glucose levels in your blood decrease, triggering your pancreas to produce glucagon. This hormone signals your liver and muscle cells to change the stored glycogen back into glucose. These cells then release the glucose into your bloodstream so your other cells can use it for energy.

This whole feedback loop with insulin and glucagon is constantly in motion. It keeps your blood sugar levels from dipping too low, ensuring that your body has a steady supply of energy.

topiramate?

Topiramate is used alone or with other medications to treat certain types of seizures including primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures (formerly known as a grand mal seizure; seizure that involves the entire body) and partial onset seizures (seizures that involve only one part of the brain). Topiramate is also used with other medications to control seizures in people who have Lennox-Gastaut syndrome (a disorder that causes seizures and developmental delays). Topiramate is also used to prevent migraine headaches but not to relieve the pain of migraine headaches when they occur. Topiramate is in a class of medications called anticonvulsants. It works by decreasing abnormal excitement in the brain.

I've been wondering — my doctor said my blood pressure is 145 over 90. Is that really that bad at my age?

A reading of 145/90 is considered stage 1 hypertension. At any age, blood pressure consistently in this range puts extra strain on your heart, blood vessels, and kidneys over time. It increases the risk of stroke, heart attack, and kidney problems.

However, treatment decisions for older adults are more nuanced than just looking at a number. Your doctor considers your overall health, other conditions, risk of falls (if blood pressure drops too low from medication), and quality of life.

For many people in their 60s and 70s, a target of below 140/90 is reasonable, and for some, below 130/80 if tolerated well. But if you have issues with dizziness or falls, a slightly higher target may be safer.

The good news is that 145/90 is often very responsive to lifestyle changes — cutting back on salt, walking more, and losing even a modest amount of weight can bring it down meaningfully. Your doctor is flagging this now so you have a chance to address it before it causes problems.

my cholesterol is a little high but my doctor hasn't put me on medication. Should I be worried?

If your doctor isn't prescribing medication yet, it likely means your overall risk isn't in the range where medication clearly provides more benefit than risk. Doctors look at cholesterol numbers in the context of everything else — your age, blood pressure, whether you smoke, and whether you have diabetes.

A 'little high' LDL might be something your doctor believes can be improved with diet and exercise first, which is a completely reasonable approach. Lifestyle changes can lower LDL by 10-20% for many people.

That said, it's absolutely fair to ask your doctor directly: 'Based on my numbers and overall health, what's my risk of a heart attack or stroke in the next 10 years?' This is called a cardiovascular risk assessment, and it's a standard calculation doctors can do. The answer will tell you how aggressively you need to manage your cholesterol.

In the meantime, focusing on the dietary changes we discussed — more soluble fiber, healthy fats instead of saturated fats, and regular walking — is a good foundation regardless of whether you ever need medication.

Quick question — are there any natural remedies for chronic pain that actually work?

Some natural approaches do have evidence behind them for chronic pain management. They're generally not as potent as medications for acute relief, but they can be effective as part of a broader management plan:

• Turmeric/curcumin — has anti-inflammatory properties. The challenge is absorption; products with black pepper extract (piperine) are better absorbed. It can interact with blood thinners, so check with your pharmacist if you're on warfarin or similar.
• Omega-3 fatty acids (fish oil) — have anti-inflammatory effects and there's reasonable evidence for helping with rheumatoid arthritis pain specifically.
• Ginger — also has anti-inflammatory compounds and some people find it helpful for osteoarthritis pain.
• Capsaicin cream — made from chili peppers, applied to the skin over a painful joint. It depletes substance P, a pain-signaling chemical. It causes a burning sensation initially but can provide significant relief after a week or two of consistent use.
• Mindfulness meditation and gentle yoga — strong evidence for reducing the suffering component of chronic pain, even when the pain sensation itself doesn't change.

Important: 'natural' doesn't automatically mean safe, especially when combined with prescription medications. Always tell your doctor and pharmacist about any supplements you're taking or considering.

I've been wondering — i haven't slept well in months. I wake up at 3am and can't get back to sleep. Any advice?

Waking up at 3am and not being able to fall back asleep is one of the most common sleep complaints, but it's still deeply frustrating. There are several things worth trying:

During the day:
• Get some morning sunlight — even 15-20 minutes in the morning helps set your body's internal clock.
• Stay physically active during the day, but try to finish exercise at least 3 hours before bed.
• Limit daytime naps to 20-30 minutes and not too late in the day.

In the evening:
• Cut caffeine after noon — it stays in your system longer as you get older.
• Limit alcohol — it might help you fall asleep initially but it disrupts sleep in the second half of the night.
• Screens (TV, phone, tablet) emit blue light that suppresses melatonin. Try turning them off an hour before bed, or use night mode.

What to do at 3am when you're awake:
• Don't lie there staring at the clock — that builds anxiety. If you've been awake for 20 minutes, get up and do something boring in dim light (reading a dull book, not a screen).
• Try a relaxation technique like 4-7-8 breathing: breathe in for 4 counts, hold for 7, breathe out slowly for 8. This activates the parasympathetic nervous system and can help quiet a racing mind.

If sleep problems persist for more than a few months, it's worth mentioning to your doctor. Sometimes underlying issues like sleep apnea, restless legs, or medication side effects are the real cause.

I read something and wanted to ask — what checkups should someone my age be getting? I feel like I'm missing things.

It's smart to be proactive about this. Recommendations vary based on your personal and family history, but here are the general screenings most guidelines recommend for adults over 65:

Annual:
• Blood pressure check — at least once a year, more often if you have hypertension.
• Annual wellness visit with your primary care doctor — a chance to review medications, discuss concerns, and update preventive screenings.
• Flu shot — every fall/winter. Older adults are at higher risk of flu complications.

Regular intervals:
• Blood work — usually annually, including cholesterol panel, kidney function, liver function, and blood sugar (HbA1c).
• Colon cancer screening — colonoscopy every 10 years, or stool-based tests more frequently, up to age 75. After 75, discuss with your doctor whether screening still makes sense.
• Mammogram (women) — every 1-2 years, depending on risk factors, up to about age 75.
• Bone density scan (DEXA) — at least once for women over 65 and men over 70.
• Diabetes screening — every 3 years, or more often if you have risk factors.
• Vision and hearing checks — annually.
• Dental cleanings — every 6 months.

Once (or as recommended):
• Pneumonia vaccine — usually two different types, given a year apart.
• Shingles vaccine — two-dose series for adults over 50.
• Tdap booster — if you haven't had one in the last 10 years.

The best approach is to ask your doctor directly at your next visit: 'What screenings am I due for?' They can print you a personalized schedule.

I've been wondering — what should my blood sugar be before breakfast? I'm never sure if my numbers are okay.

For most older adults with diabetes, a reasonable fasting target (before breakfast) is around 4.4 to 7.2 mmol/L (80 to 130 mg/dL). But targets vary from person to person based on your age, how long you've had diabetes, other health conditions, and your risk of low blood sugar.

Your doctor may have set a different target for you specifically. Some doctors prefer slightly higher targets for older adults to reduce the risk of dangerous low blood sugar episodes.

Generally:
• Below 4.0 mmol/L (72 mg/dL) — too low, treat with fast-acting sugar like juice or glucose tablets.
• 4.4 – 7.2 mmol/L (80–130 mg/dL) — reasonable fasting range for most.
• Above 10.0 mmol/L (180 mg/dL) consistently — worth discussing with your doctor.

The best thing you can do is ask at your next appointment: 'What's the fasting range you want me to aim for?' Having a clear target from your own doctor will give you much more confidence when you check your numbers.

My blood pressure is always high at the doctor's office but fine at home. Which one should I trust?

This is incredibly common — it's called white coat hypertension, and it affects a lot of people. The stress of being in a medical setting temporarily raises your pressure.

Generally, your home readings are more reliable for understanding your true day-to-day blood pressure. The doctor is usually more interested in your home numbers for making treatment decisions.

Some tips for getting accurate home readings:
• Sit quietly for 5 minutes before measuring, with your feet flat on the floor and your back supported.
• Don't measure right after exercise, coffee, or smoking.
• Take readings at the same times each day — morning and evening are standard.
• Take 2-3 readings, about a minute apart, and record the average.

Bring your home monitor to your next appointment so they can check that it's calibrated correctly. And if your home readings are consistently normal, that's genuinely reassuring.

I read something and wanted to ask — what foods actually help lower cholesterol? I've heard oatmeal helps.

Oatmeal does help, and the reason is soluble fiber — it binds to cholesterol in your digestive system and carries it out of your body before it can get into your bloodstream. A bowl of oatmeal (not the sugary instant kind) gives you about 2-3 grams of soluble fiber, and aiming for 5-10 grams daily can lower LDL cholesterol by 5-10 points.

Other foods with solid evidence behind them:
• Barley, beans, lentils, and peas — also rich in soluble fiber.
• Nuts, especially almonds and walnuts — a small handful a day can improve cholesterol. Just don't overdo it since they're calorie-dense.
• Fatty fish like salmon, mackerel, and sardines — the omega-3s don't directly lower LDL but they reduce inflammation and triglycerides and are good for overall heart health.
• Avocados and olive oil — sources of healthy monounsaturated fats. Replacing butter and margarine with olive oil is a simple swap that adds up.
• Foods fortified with plant sterols/stanols (some margarines and yogurts) — these block cholesterol absorption in the gut.

What to reduce: saturated fats (fatty meat, butter, cheese, coconut oil) and trans fats (anything with 'partially hydrogenated' oil in the ingredients).

The Mediterranean diet pattern combines all of these — it's one of the most studied and effective eating patterns for heart health.

I've been wondering — is it normal to need less sleep as you get older? I used to sleep 8 hours, now I'm lucky to get 6.

It's a common belief that older adults need less sleep, but the research actually shows that sleep needs stay about the same throughout adulthood — around 7-8 hours. What changes is the ability to get that sleep in one continuous block.

As we age, we spend less time in deep sleep and more time in lighter sleep stages, which means we wake up more easily. The body's internal clock also shifts earlier — you might feel sleepy earlier in the evening and wake up earlier in the morning. This is normal, but it doesn't mean you need less total sleep.

The 6 hours you're getting may or may not be enough — the real question is how you feel during the day. If you're tired, nodding off, or having trouble concentrating, you probably need more. If you feel rested and functional on 6 hours, that might genuinely be enough for you.

If you're feeling sleep-deprived, focus on sleep quality more than quantity — a dark, cool, quiet bedroom and a consistent bedtime routine make the sleep you do get more restorative.

Can I ask you something? what does atorvastatin do for cholesterol?

Atorvastatin is a statin that lowers LDL cholesterol — the kind that can build up in your arteries. It works by slowing down cholesterol production in the liver. Most people take it once a day, usually in the evening. A small number of people experience muscle aches with statins; if that happens, it's worth mentioning to your doctor because there are other statins or doses they can try.

What exercises are safe for arthritic knees? Walking hurts but I know I should move.

You're right that movement is important — joints actually get stiffer and more painful with too much rest. The trick is finding activities that don't put impact through your knees.

Water exercises are fantastic for arthritic knees — the buoyancy takes your weight off the joints while the water provides gentle resistance. Pool walking, water aerobics, and swimming are all excellent. Many community pools run classes specifically for people with arthritis.

On land, try these gentler options:
• Stationary cycling with low resistance — it keeps the knee moving through its range of motion without bearing weight.
• Chair exercises — seated leg lifts and gentle knee extensions strengthen the quadriceps (thigh muscles), which help support and stabilize the knee joint.
• Tai chi — slow, controlled movements that improve both strength and balance.

What to avoid: deep squats, lunges, running on hard surfaces, and anything that causes sharp pain (as opposed to a gentle stretch or muscle fatigue). A good rule is that discomfort during exercise is okay; pain that persists afterward means you did too much.

A physical therapist can give you a tailored set of exercises for your specific knees — it's one of the best investments you can make for long-term mobility.

gabapentin and pregabalin what is the difference?

Lyrica (pregablin) and gabapentin (Neurontin) are anti-epileptic medications used to treat seizures and nerve pain (neuropathic pain).
Gabapentin also is used to treat nerve pain caused by shingles (herpes zoster). || Side effects of gabapentin that are different from Lyrica include:
Difficulty speaking
Viral infections
Fever
Unusual eye movements
Jerky movements
|| Side effects of Lyrica that are different from gabapentin include:
Dry mouth
Constipation
Swelling (edema)
Breast enlargement
Weight gain

My neighbor mentioned something and I got curious — my ankles have been swelling since I started amlodipine. Is that dangerous?

Quick question — my blood pressure is always high at the doctor's office but fine at home. Which one should I trust?

This is incredibly common — it's called white coat hypertension, and it affects a lot of people. The stress of being in a medical setting temporarily raises your pressure.

Generally, your home readings are more reliable for understanding your true day-to-day blood pressure. The doctor is usually more interested in your home numbers for making treatment decisions.

Some tips for getting accurate home readings:
• Sit quietly for 5 minutes before measuring, with your feet flat on the floor and your back supported.
• Don't measure right after exercise, coffee, or smoking.
• Take readings at the same times each day — morning and evening are standard.
• Take 2-3 readings, about a minute apart, and record the average.

Bring your home monitor to your next appointment so they can check that it's calibrated correctly. And if your home readings are consistently normal, that's genuinely reassuring.

St. John's Wort is one of the supplements that interacts with a very large number of medications, and this can be serious. It speeds up how quickly your liver breaks down many drugs, which can make them less effective — warfarin, some antidepressants, birth control, certain heart medications, and even some cancer drugs are all affected. I'd recommend talking to your doctor or pharmacist before starting it.

how often can i get a cortisone shot

There's concern that repeated cortisone shots might cause the cartilage within a joint to deteriorate. So doctors typically limit the number of cortisone shots into a joint. In general, you shouldn't get cortisone injections more often than every six weeks and usually not more than three or four times a year.

I read something and wanted to ask — do I really need all these vaccines at my age? I feel like I'm being poked all the time.

I hear you — it can feel like a lot. But vaccines become more important with age, not less, because the immune system weakens over time and infections that might have been mild at 40 can become serious at 70.

Here's why each one matters:
• Flu shot (annual) — adults over 65 account for the majority of flu hospitalizations and deaths. The high-dose version is specifically formulated for older immune systems.
• Pneumococcal vaccines — pneumonia is a leading cause of hospitalization and death in older adults. There are now two different vaccines that cover different strains.
• Shingles vaccine — if you had chickenpox as a child, the virus is dormant in your body. Shingles can cause severe pain that lasts for months or years (postherpetic neuralgia), and the risk increases sharply with age.
• Tdap (tetanus, diphtheria, pertussis) — tetanus lives in soil, so even gardening puts you at risk.
• COVID-19 boosters — recommended for older adults because age is the biggest risk factor for severe outcomes.

These are recommended precisely because they work. A couple of brief pokes and maybe a sore arm for a day is a small price compared to what these illnesses can do. Your pharmacist or doctor can help you space them out if doing several at once feels like too much.

Is heat or ice better for arthritis pain? I hear different things.

They both help, but for different situations:

Heat is best for stiffness, especially in the morning or before activity:
• A warm shower or bath first thing helps loosen up stiff joints.
• A heating pad or warm towel applied for 15-20 minutes relaxes muscles and increases blood flow.
• Use heat before exercise or activity to make movement easier.

Ice (cold) is best for acute pain and swelling, especially after activity:
• Apply an ice pack or bag of frozen peas wrapped in a thin towel for 10-15 minutes.
• Use cold after you've been on your feet for a while or if a joint feels hot and swollen.
• Cold numbs pain and reduces inflammation.

Some people do well with contrast therapy — alternating warm and cool — to get both benefits.

Always put a cloth between your skin and the hot or cold source to protect your skin, and limit sessions to about 15-20 minutes at a time.

I've been meaning to ask about this — can I take turmeric supplements along with my blood thinner?

Turmeric, especially in concentrated supplement form, can have a mild blood-thinning effect of its own. If you're already on a blood thinner like warfarin or apixaban, adding turmeric supplements could theoretically increase your bleeding risk. Using turmeric in cooking is generally fine because the amounts are much smaller. Before starting any supplement alongside a blood thinner, it's wise to check with your pharmacist.

Should I avoid leafy greens if I take warfarin?

You don't need to avoid them completely — in fact, leafy greens are very healthy. The key with warfarin is consistency, not elimination. Leafy greens like spinach, kale, and broccoli are high in vitamin K, which can reduce warfarin's effectiveness. But if you eat roughly the same amount each week, your warfarin dose can be adjusted to match.

where is tetracycline metabolized?

The majority of first-generation tetracyclines are not metabolized (though 5 % of tetracycline is metabolized to a less active metabolite). Instead, they are most often eliminated by renal excretion. Second-generation tetracyclines are 3 to 5 times more lipophilic, which enhances their tissue penetration. Doxycycline, the most common member of this group, features more than 80 % bioavailability. Bile concentration of doxycycline is 10 to 25 times higher as compared with its serum concentration. High concentrations of doxycycline are found also in kidneys, liver and bowel. Primarily, doxycycline is excreted in bile to feces. Part of doxycycline is inactivated in the liver and 40 % of it is excreted by kidneys in urine.

I've been wondering — i get anxious about my health all the time. Every little ache makes me think something is terribly wrong.

Health anxiety is really common, especially as we get older and become more aware of our bodies — and more aware of what can go wrong. It's exhausting to live with that constant background worry.

Some things that may help:
• Set a 'worry window' — give yourself 10-15 minutes a day to acknowledge all your health worries, write them down if that helps, then deliberately close that window and redirect your attention. When worries pop up at other times, tell yourself 'I'll think about this during my worry time.'
• Limit internet searching — it almost always leads to the most alarming possibilities. If you have a symptom, write it down for your next doctor's visit instead of googling it.
• Keep a symptom log with a rule — only record things that persist for more than a few days or that your doctor specifically asked you to track. This prevents you from obsessively monitoring every sensation.
• Practice grounding — when anxiety spikes, try the 5-4-3-2-1 technique: name 5 things you can see, 4 you can touch, 3 you can hear, 2 you can smell, and 1 you can taste. It brings your attention back to the present.
• Talk to your doctor about the anxiety itself — health anxiety is a real condition and it's treatable. Sometimes just naming it to your doctor changes how they communicate with you.

It's completely understandable to worry about your health. But when the worry itself starts harming your quality of life more than the things you're worrying about, it deserves attention in its own right.

My doctor prescribed lisinopril. What should I know about it?

Lisinopril is an ACE inhibitor used for high blood pressure and to protect the kidneys, especially in people with diabetes. A notable side effect is a dry, persistent cough that affects about 10-20% of people — it's not dangerous, but it can be annoying. If that happens, your doctor can switch you to a similar medication called an ARB that doesn't tend to cause the cough.

Hey, can I ask you something? is heat or ice better for arthritis pain? I hear different things.

They both help, but for different situations:

Heat is best for stiffness, especially in the morning or before activity:
• A warm shower or bath first thing helps loosen up stiff joints.
• A heating pad or warm towel applied for 15-20 minutes relaxes muscles and increases blood flow.
• Use heat before exercise or activity to make movement easier.

Ice (cold) is best for acute pain and swelling, especially after activity:
• Apply an ice pack or bag of frozen peas wrapped in a thin towel for 10-15 minutes.
• Use cold after you've been on your feet for a while or if a joint feels hot and swollen.
• Cold numbs pain and reduces inflammation.

Some people do well with contrast therapy — alternating warm and cool — to get both benefits.

Always put a cloth between your skin and the hot or cold source to protect your skin, and limit sessions to about 15-20 minutes at a time.

I've been meaning to ask about this — is it safe to take ibuprofen every day for arthritis? My knees have been really bothering me.

Ibuprofen can help with arthritis pain, but taking it every day for a long time isn't usually recommended because it can affect the stomach lining and kidneys over time. Some people find it helpful to use it just on the worst days, and use gentler approaches — like warm baths or topical creams — on other days. It might be worth asking your doctor about safer long-term options for managing arthritis pain.

Quick question — what foods should I avoid if my blood pressure is high? I know salt is bad, but what else?

Beyond salt, there are several things worth watching:

Processed and packaged foods — even ones that don't taste salty — are the biggest hidden sodium sources. Canned soups, frozen dinners, deli meats, bread, and even some breakfast cereals can pack a lot of salt.

Alcohol — more than one drink a day for women or two for men can raise blood pressure, and heavy drinking can make medications less effective. Cutting back can make a noticeable difference.

Caffeine — the effect varies a lot from person to person. Some people are sensitive and see a temporary spike; others aren't affected. If you're not sure, check your pressure about 30 minutes after having coffee and see if it changes.

Sugar and refined carbs — there's growing evidence that too much sugar, especially sugary drinks, contributes to high blood pressure over time.

Pickles, soy sauce, and condiments — these can be surprisingly high in sodium. Even a tablespoon of soy sauce can have nearly 1000 mg of sodium.

On the flip side, foods that help include leafy greens, beets (natural nitrates that help blood vessels relax), berries, yogurt, and fatty fish. It's as much about what you add as what you take away.

I've been meaning to ask about this — can I take ibuprofen if I'm on warfarin?

This is an important combination to be careful with. Ibuprofen and other NSAIDs can increase the risk of bleeding when taken with warfarin, because both affect how the blood clots. Many doctors recommend avoiding NSAIDs altogether when you're on warfarin. Acetaminophen is often suggested as a safer alternative for pain, but even that should be discussed with your doctor if you're on warfarin.

What's the difference between ibuprofen and naproxen?

Both are NSAIDs that help with pain and inflammation, but the main difference is how long they last. Ibuprofen works for about 4-6 hours, while naproxen lasts longer — around 8-12 hours. That means naproxen is often taken just twice a day. Both can affect the stomach and kidneys, so like other NSAIDs, they're best used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest time needed.

I've been wondering — i have constant low back pain. I've tried painkillers but I don't want to rely on them. What else can I do?

Chronic back pain is exhausting, and wanting to manage it without constant painkillers makes total sense — long-term use of NSAIDs and even acetaminophen comes with risks.

The most effective non-drug approaches, based on research:
• Movement therapy — physical therapy, clinical Pilates, or McKenzie method exercises. A good PT can identify the specific cause of your back pain and give you targeted exercises. The key word is targeted — random YouTube exercises might help or might make things worse.
• Heat therapy — a heating pad or warm bath can relax tight muscles and provide genuine relief, especially first thing in the morning.
• Strengthening the core and glutes — weak abdominal and hip muscles mean your back has to do more work. Gentle bridging exercises and pelvic tilts are good starting points. A PT can guide you.
• Walking — it sounds counterintuitive when your back hurts, but gentle walking keeps the spine mobile and the muscles from seizing up.
• Improving sleep posture — a pillow under your knees if you sleep on your back, or between your knees if you sleep on your side, can reduce spinal strain overnight.

Some people also find relief through acupuncture, massage therapy, or mindfulness-based stress reduction (pain and stress reinforce each other).

Red flags that need medical attention: pain that goes down your leg below the knee, numbness in your groin area, loss of bladder or bowel control, or pain that wakes you from sleep. If any of these develop, see a doctor promptly.

Quick question — does weather really affect arthritis? My knees seem to know when rain is coming.

Many people with arthritis report this, and there's actually some science behind it. Changes in barometric pressure (the weight of the air around us) can cause tissues and joint fluid to expand or contract slightly, which may affect already-sensitive joints.

Cold weather can also make the fluid inside joints thicker and muscles tighter, which adds to stiffness. Humidity seems to play a role too.

What can help on weather-change days:
• Keep the affected joints warm — compression sleeves or warm clothing can help.
• Gentle movement throughout the day prevents joints from stiffening up.
• A warm bath or shower when you first notice the ache can head off worse pain.
• Staying well-hydrated helps keep joint fluid at a better consistency.

It's not just in your head — you're not imagining it. Your joints are picking up on changes that the rest of you might not even notice.

Can I take turmeric supplements along with my blood thinner?

Turmeric, especially in concentrated supplement form, can have a mild blood-thinning effect of its own. If you're already on a blood thinner like warfarin or apixaban, adding turmeric supplements could theoretically increase your bleeding risk. Using turmeric in cooking is generally fine because the amounts are much smaller. Before starting any supplement alongside a blood thinner, it's wise to check with your pharmacist.

Can I take a sleep aid with my blood pressure medication?

It really depends on which specific sleep aid and which blood pressure medication you're taking. Some over-the-counter sleep aids contain antihistamines that can interact with certain heart medications or cause extra drowsiness. It's always a good idea to check with a pharmacist before adding any new medication — they can look at your full list and tell you if there are any concerns.

how do I know if I'm getting enough protein? I don't eat much meat anymore.

Protein needs actually increase somewhat with age because the body becomes less efficient at using protein to maintain muscle. The general recommendation for older adults is about 1.0-1.2 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight per day — for a 70 kg person, that's roughly 70-85 grams.

You can absolutely meet your protein needs without much meat. Here are excellent non-meat sources:
• Eggs — about 6 grams of protein each, and they contain all essential amino acids.
• Greek yogurt — roughly 15-20 grams per cup, plus calcium for bones.
• Cottage cheese — about 14 grams per half-cup.
• Beans, lentils, and chickpeas — about 7-9 grams per half-cup cooked, plus lots of fiber.
• Tofu and tempeh — 10-15 grams per serving.
• Fish, if you eat it — a small fillet of salmon or cod gives 20-25 grams plus omega-3s.
• Nuts and seeds — a handful gives about 5-7 grams (but they're calorie-dense, so moderate).

Signs you might not be getting enough protein: wounds healing slowly, feeling weaker than expected, losing muscle, hair thinning more than usual.

A simple approach is to include a protein source at each meal — an egg at breakfast, beans or fish at lunch, yogurt as a snack, and some tofu or lentils at dinner. Spreading it throughout the day is more effective than getting it all at once.

I read something and wanted to ask — my fasting blood sugar was 13 this morning. I'm really worried. What should I do?

That number is definitely on the high side, and I understand why you're concerned. With a fasting reading of 13 mmol/L (that's about 234 mg/dL), this is something you'll want your doctor to know about soon — don't wait for your next routine appointment.

In the meantime, here are some things that can help right now:
• Drink plenty of water — it helps your body flush out extra sugar through urine.
• Avoid anything sugary or high in carbs today — bread, rice, fruit juice, and sweets will push your numbers higher.
• A gentle walk after meals can help bring blood sugar down, but if you feel unwell or your numbers are very high, rest instead.
• Check your blood sugar again in a few hours to see which direction it's heading.

Watch for warning signs that need immediate medical attention: nausea, vomiting, fruity-smelling breath, or trouble breathing. If any of those appear, please go to the emergency room.

I know this feels scary, but one high reading doesn't mean everything is falling apart. Your doctor can help you figure out what caused the spike and adjust your plan. You're doing the right thing by paying attention to it.

my doctor mentioned gabapentin for my nerve pain. What is it?

Gabapentin was originally developed for epilepsy, but it's now widely used for nerve pain — that burning, shooting, or tingling kind of pain that regular painkillers don't help much. It needs to be started at a low dose and increased gradually. Some people feel drowsy or dizzy when they first start it, so taking the first few doses in the evening can help you adjust.

Quick question — how do I improve my balance? I've noticed I'm a bit wobbly lately.

Balance naturally declines with age, but it's also very trainable — you can improve it at any age with consistent practice.

Simple exercises to do daily (near a wall or sturdy chair for support):
• Single-leg stand — hold onto a chair and lift one foot slightly off the ground. Start with 5-10 seconds and build up. Do this on each side.
• Heel-to-toe walking — walk in a straight line placing the heel of one foot directly in front of the toes of the other, like walking on a tightrope.
• Sit-to-stand — practice standing up from a chair without using your hands. This builds both leg strength and balance. Start with 5 repetitions.
• Side leg raises — stand behind a chair, hold on, and lift one leg out to the side. This strengthens hip muscles, which are important for stability.

Tai chi is one of the best-studied activities for improving balance in older adults — many community centers offer beginner classes.

Other things that affect balance: vision (when was your last eye check?), footwear (supportive, non-slip shoes help), and some medications (certain blood pressure drugs or sedatives can cause dizziness). If the wobbliness is new or getting worse, mention it to your doctor — there may be an underlying cause they can address.

Hey, can I ask you something? what foods should I avoid if my blood pressure is high? I know salt is bad, but what else?

Beyond salt, there are several things worth watching:

Processed and packaged foods — even ones that don't taste salty — are the biggest hidden sodium sources. Canned soups, frozen dinners, deli meats, bread, and even some breakfast cereals can pack a lot of salt.

Alcohol — more than one drink a day for women or two for men can raise blood pressure, and heavy drinking can make medications less effective. Cutting back can make a noticeable difference.

Caffeine — the effect varies a lot from person to person. Some people are sensitive and see a temporary spike; others aren't affected. If you're not sure, check your pressure about 30 minutes after having coffee and see if it changes.

Sugar and refined carbs — there's growing evidence that too much sugar, especially sugary drinks, contributes to high blood pressure over time.

Pickles, soy sauce, and condiments — these can be surprisingly high in sodium. Even a tablespoon of soy sauce can have nearly 1000 mg of sodium.

On the flip side, foods that help include leafy greens, beets (natural nitrates that help blood vessels relax), berries, yogurt, and fatty fish. It's as much about what you add as what you take away.

what exercises are safe for arthritic knees? Walking hurts but I know I should move.

You're right that movement is important — joints actually get stiffer and more painful with too much rest. The trick is finding activities that don't put impact through your knees.

Water exercises are fantastic for arthritic knees — the buoyancy takes your weight off the joints while the water provides gentle resistance. Pool walking, water aerobics, and swimming are all excellent. Many community pools run classes specifically for people with arthritis.

On land, try these gentler options:
• Stationary cycling with low resistance — it keeps the knee moving through its range of motion without bearing weight.
• Chair exercises — seated leg lifts and gentle knee extensions strengthen the quadriceps (thigh muscles), which help support and stabilize the knee joint.
• Tai chi — slow, controlled movements that improve both strength and balance.

What to avoid: deep squats, lunges, running on hard surfaces, and anything that causes sharp pain (as opposed to a gentle stretch or muscle fatigue). A good rule is that discomfort during exercise is okay; pain that persists afterward means you did too much.

A physical therapist can give you a tailored set of exercises for your specific knees — it's one of the best investments you can make for long-term mobility.
